META.subject: Mathematics
META.substrand_name: Surface Area and Volume of Solids
META.titleDoc: MATHEMATICS GRADE 10: SURFACE AREA AND VOLUME OF SOLIDS
META.subtitleDoc: CBE Phenomenon-Driven Lesson Sequence — Sub-Strand 2.4 (10 Lessons)

UNIT.subject: Mathematics
UNIT.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.7: Surface Area and Volume of Solids
UNIT.content:
• Surface area of prisms (triangular, rectangular, and other prisms)
• Surface area of pyramids (triangular and rectangular-based pyramids)
• Surface area of cones
• Surface area of spheres
• Surface area of frustums of a cone
• Surface area of frustums of a pyramid
• Surface area of composite solids
• Volume of prisms (triangular, rectangular, and other prisms)
• Volume of pyramids (triangular and rectangular-based pyramids)
• Volume of cones
• Volume of spheres
• Volume of frustums of a cone
• Volume of frustums of a pyramid
• Volume of composite solids
• Real-life applications of surface area and volume of solids
UNIT.learningOutcomes:
By the end of the sub-strand, the learner should be able to:
a) determine the surface area of prisms, pyramids, cones, and spheres in different situations,
b) determine the surface area of frustums of a cone and a pyramid in different situations,
c) calculate the surface area of composite solids in real-life contexts,
d) calculate the volume of prisms, pyramids, cones, and spheres in different situations,
e) determine the volume of frustums of a cone and a pyramid in different situations,
f) determine the volume of composite solids in real-life contexts,
g) apply surface area and volume of solids to solve real-life problems,
h) appreciate the use of surface area and volume of solids in day-to-day activities.
UNIT.coreCompetencies:
• Communication and Collaboration: the learner discusses with peers and works in groups to derive and verify formulae for surface area and volume of various solids, sharing findings and explaining reasoning to classmates.
• Critical Thinking and Problem Solving: the learner applies knowledge of surface area and volume to solve real-life problems such as estimating the amount of material needed to construct a grain storage silo or a water tank in the Rift Valley.
• Digital Literacy: the learner uses digital devices to access YouTube videos on surface area and volume of frustums and composite solids, and uses calculators to verify computed values.
• Learning to Learn: the learner builds on prior knowledge of area and volume of basic shapes to extend understanding progressively to frustums and composite solids.
• Self-efficacy: the learner develops confidence in applying mathematical formulae to multi-step problems involving composite solids encountered in everyday Kenyan contexts.
UNIT.values:
• Responsibility: the learner takes care of mathematical instruments, calculators, and digital devices used during investigations of surface area and volume.
• Respect: the learner listens to and values the contributions of peers during group discussions on the properties and measurements of solids.
• Unity: the learner works collaboratively in groups to measure, compute, and verify surface areas and volumes of physical models of solids.
• Social Justice: the learner recognises how accurate computation of surface area and volume contributes to fair and efficient use of resources, such as equitable distribution of grain storage facilities across counties in Kenya.
• Integrity: the learner records and reports measurements and calculations honestly, without falsifying results during practical investigations.
UNIT.sep:
• Developing and Using Models: learners construct and manipulate physical nets and three-dimensional models of prisms, pyramids, cones, and frustums to derive surface area formulae, then revise their models as understanding deepens.
• Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking: learners apply geometric formulae, substitute values, and use calculators to compute surface areas and volumes accurately, comparing results across different solid types.
• Analysing and Interpreting Data: learners measure physical objects (e.g., a cylindrical uji pot, a conical grain store, a rectangular water tank) and analyse whether computed values match real-world capacity labels.
• Constructing Explanations: learners explain, using derived formulae and worked examples, why a frustum has a larger surface area than a cone of the same base and slant height, and how composite solids combine individual formulae.
• Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information: learners watch curated YouTube videos on frustum surface area and volume, evaluate the methods demonstrated, and communicate their understanding through poster presentations and model demonstrations.
UNIT.pcis:
• Life Skills (Creative Thinking): the learner designs a composite solid container (e.g., a water storage tank combining a cylinder and hemisphere) and computes its surface area and volume, applying creative thinking to a real Kenyan engineering context.
• Life Skills (Problem Solving): the learner solves multi-step problems involving the surface area and volume of solids encountered in everyday situations, such as estimating the cost of painting a conical roof or filling a frustum-shaped grain silo in Kitale.
• Environmental Education: the learner explores how efficient design of solids (minimising surface area for a given volume) contributes to sustainable use of materials, such as reducing the amount of metal sheet used to manufacture water tanks for rural communities in Turkana County.
• Financial Literacy: the learner calculates the cost of materials needed to construct or cover solids, applying unit cost to computed surface areas to estimate project budgets relevant to small businesses and farming enterprises in Kenya.
UNIT.careers:
• Architecture and Construction: architects and civil engineers in Kenya use surface area and volume calculations to design buildings, estimate materials, and price construction projects such as grain silos, water tanks, and domed rooftops.
• Manufacturing and Industrial Design: manufacturers of containers, packaging, and storage facilities (e.g., metal fabricators in Industrial Area, Nairobi) rely on surface area formulae to minimise material waste and optimise production costs.
• Agriculture and Agribusiness: agronomists and farmers in the Rift Valley calculate the volume of conical or cylindrical grain stores and water reservoirs to manage harvests and irrigation efficiently.
• Medicine and Health Sciences: medical professionals and biomedical engineers use volume calculations of spherical and cylindrical vessels when designing pharmaceutical containers, IV drip bags, and medical implants.
• Water Engineering and Environmental Science: water engineers designing dams, tanks, and pipelines for counties such as Makueni and Baringo apply volume and surface area knowledge to ensure adequate water storage and distribution.
• Art and Graphic Design: Kenyan artists and product designers use geometric solid properties when creating sculptures, jewellery moulds, and product packaging.
UNIT.focus: This unit focuses on equipping Grade 10 learners with the knowledge and skills to compute the surface area and volume of a wide range of three-dimensional solids — including prisms, pyramids, cones, spheres, frustums, and composite solids — and to apply these computations in authentic Kenyan real-life contexts such as designing grain stores, water tanks, and roofing structures. The unit progresses systematically from familiar solids (prisms and pyramids) through curved surfaces (cones and spheres) to more complex shapes (frustums and composite solids), building conceptual understanding at each stage. A phenomenon-driven, collaborative approach is used throughout, with learners constructing physical nets, watching curated video demonstrations, and solving contextualised problems to develop both procedural fluency and deep conceptual understanding.
UNIT.phenomenon: ANCHORING PHENOMENON: A water harvesting tank manufacturer in Kisumu produces two types of storage tanks for rural households in Siaya County: a cylindrical tank topped with a hemispherical lid, and a frustum-shaped tank (wider at the bottom, narrower at the top). Both tanks are advertised as holding exactly 5,000 litres of water. When a group of Grade 10 students visits the factory, they notice that the frustum tank uses noticeably less metal sheet than the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank, yet holds the same volume of water. The students are puzzled: if both tanks hold the same amount of water (same volume), why does one require significantly less material to build (smaller surface area)? This observation drives the learners to investigate how the shape of a solid determines its surface area independently of its volume, and how manufacturers and engineers in Kenya strategically choose solid shapes to minimise cost while maximising storage capacity.
UNIT.supportingPhenomena:
• A mandazi vendor in Mombasa's Old Town deep-fries mandazi dough cut into different shapes — spherical balls, conical rolls, and flat triangular prisms — and notices that the spherical mandazi browns more evenly and absorbs less oil. Learners investigate how the surface-area-to-volume ratio of each shape explains the frying behaviour and oil absorption.
• A farmer in Kitale stores harvested maize in two grain stores: one is a rectangular block structure and the other is a conical thatched silo of the same height. The conical silo holds less grain than expected. Learners compute and compare the volumes of both structures to explain the difference in storage capacity.
• A Kenyan artisan in Kericho crafts tea canister gift sets by soldering flat metal sheets into composite solid shapes (cylinder + cone lid). Learners are given a flat net of the canister and must calculate the exact area of metal sheet required — connecting the net (2D surface) to total surface area (3D solid).
• Workers at a construction site in Nairobi's Westlands are painting a roof consisting of four conical turrets and a central hemispherical dome. The foreman needs to purchase the correct amount of paint. Learners calculate the lateral surface area of each conical turret and the curved surface area of the dome to determine the total area to be painted and the litres of paint required.
UNIT.drivingQuestion:
DRIVING QUESTION: How does the shape of a solid determine how much material is needed to build it and how much it can hold — and how do engineers and manufacturers in Kenya use this knowledge to design efficient, cost-effective structures?

KICD KEY INQUIRY QUESTIONS:
1. How do we apply surface area and volume of solids in day-to-day activities?
2. How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume?
UNIT.storylineThread:
Lesson 1 — Surface Area of Prisms and Pyramids: Learners are introduced to the anchoring phenomenon (the Kisumu water tank factory) and make initial predictions about why the two tanks use different amounts of metal. They then derive and apply formulae for the surface area of triangular and rectangular prisms and pyramids using physical nets, grounding all calculations in the context of flat metal sheets being cut and folded at the factory.

Lesson 2 — Surface Area of Cones and Spheres: Learners extend their investigation to curved solids, deriving the lateral and total surface area formulae for cones and spheres. They connect this to the supporting phenomenon of spherical mandazi and conical roof turrets in Nairobi, reinforcing why curved surfaces have distinct formulae from flat-faced solids.

Lesson 3 — Surface Area of Frustums of a Cone: Using the YouTube video on the surface area of a frustum of a cone, learners investigate how cutting a cone parallel to its base produces a frustum. They derive the surface area formula for a cone frustum and apply it to the anchoring phenomenon — calculating the surface area of the frustum-shaped water tank in Kisumu.

Lesson 4 — Surface Area of Frustums of a Pyramid: Building on Lesson 3, learners watch the YouTube video on the surface area of a frustum of a pyramid and derive its formula. They apply this to contextualised problems such as calculating the metal sheet needed for a frustum-shaped grain store in Kitale, updating the Driving Question Board with new understanding.

Lesson 5 — Surface Area of Composite Solids: Learners watch the YouTube video on calculating the surface area of composite solids and apply a systematic strategy (identify components, subtract hidden faces, sum exposed surfaces) to compute the surface area of composite solids. They apply this to the Kericho tea canister and the cylinder-hemisphere water tank from the anchoring phenomenon, directly comparing it to the frustum tank from Lessons 3–4.

Lesson 6 — Volume of Prisms, Pyramids, Cones, and Spheres: Learners shift focus from surface area to volume, deriving and applying formulae for the volume of prisms, pyramids, cones, and spheres. Using the Kitale maize storage supporting phenomenon, they compute and compare volumes of rectangular and conical grain stores, explaining the farmer's observation about differing storage capacities.

Lesson 7 — Volume of Frustums of a Cone: Using the YouTube video on the volume of a frustum of a cone, learners derive the frustum volume formula from the difference of two cones. They calculate the volume of the frustum-shaped water tank from the anchoring phenomenon, confirming whether it truly holds 5,000 litres, and update the DQB to track the growing resolution of the driving question.

Lesson 8 — Volume of Frustums of a Pyramid: Building on Lesson 7, learners watch the YouTube video on the volume of a frustum of a pyramid and derive its formula. They apply it to problems involving pyramid-frustum grain stores and architectural structures, connecting to careers in construction and agribusiness in Kenya.

Lesson 9 — Volume of Composite Solids: Using the YouTube video on the volume of composite solids, learners apply a decomposition strategy to compute volumes of composite solids (e.g., cylinder + hemisphere water tank, cone + cylinder silo). They revisit the anchoring phenomenon in full — computing both the surface area and volume of both tank designs side by side — to finally explain why the frustum tank uses less material while holding the same volume.

Lesson 10 — Real-Life Applications and Model Building: Learners consolidate their understanding by solving a rich real-life task set in a Kenyan context (e.g., designing the most cost-efficient water storage tank for a school in Siaya County). They present their final annotated cumulative models, update the Driving Question Board with complete explanations, and reflect on how surface area and volume of solids are used across careers in architecture, agriculture, manufacturing, and water engineering in Kenya.

LESSONS.title: Surface Area of Prisms and Pyramids — Launching the Phenomenon
LESSONS.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.4: Surface Area and Volume of Solids

slo.purpose: To introduce learners to the anchoring phenomenon of the Kisumu water tank factory and establish foundational understanding of surface area by deriving and applying formulae for prisms and pyramids through physical nets.
slo.knowledge: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) define surface area as the total area of all faces of a 3D solid; (b) identify and sketch nets of triangular prisms, rectangular prisms, and pyramids; (c) derive surface area formulae for triangular prisms, rectangular prisms, and square-based pyramids; (d) calculate the surface area of prisms and pyramids in real-world contexts.
slo.skills: The learner should be able to: (a) construct and interpret nets of 3D solids to determine total surface area; (b) apply the correct surface area formula to solve problems related to material estimation; (c) communicate mathematical reasoning clearly using correct terminology.
slo.attitudes: The learner should: (a) appreciate how geometric reasoning supports efficient, cost-effective engineering and manufacturing decisions in Kenya; (b) show curiosity and persistence when investigating why different shapes use different amounts of material for the same volume.
slo.keyInquiry: How do we apply surface area and volume of solids in day-to-day activities? | How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the opening lesson of the sub-strand. Learners are introduced to the anchoring phenomenon — a water tank factory in Kisumu producing a cylindrical-hemispherical tank and a frustum tank, both holding 5,000 litres, but using different amounts of metal sheet. Learners make initial predictions about the phenomenon, then build foundational surface area skills using prisms and pyramids modelled as flat metal sheets being cut and folded at the factory. This lesson opens the Driving Question Board and positions learners to investigate how shape determines surface area independently of volume across the unit.
slo.safetyNotes: Learners using scissors to cut nets should handle them with care and pass them blade-down. No other significant hazards anticipated. Ensure adequate desk space for net construction activities.

LESSONS.overview: Learners open the sub-strand by encountering the anchoring phenomenon: a water tank factory in Kisumu manufactures two tanks — a composite cylinder-hemisphere and a frustum — that both hold exactly 5,000 litres, but the frustum uses noticeably less metal sheet. After recording initial predictions, learners connect this puzzle to surface area. They explore physical nets of rectangular prisms, triangular prisms, and square-based pyramids — modelled as flat metal sheets being cut and folded — to derive surface area formulae. Learners apply these formulae to factory-relevant problems, update their initial predictions, and co-create the opening Driving Question Board. The lesson closes with a cumulative class model that maps 'shape → net → surface area formula' as the first layer of the unit's conceptual model.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a large photograph (or teacher-drawn diagram on the board) of two tanks side by side at a factory in Kisumu: Tank A is a tall cylinder with a hemispherical dome on top; Tank B is a frustum (wide base, narrower top). A label on each reads: 'Capacity: 5,000 litres.' A factory worker speech bubble says: 'Tank B always uses less metal sheet than Tank A — but both hold the same water!' Learners individually write a 3-sentence prediction in their exercise books answering: 'Why do you think Tank B uses less metal sheet than Tank A even though both hold 5,000 litres?' They annotate their prediction with a rough sketch. After 4 minutes of silent individual writing, learners share predictions with a shoulder partner for 2 minutes. Four learners are cold-called to share with the class.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the tank diagram/photograph prominently. Read the scenario aloud dramatically: 'Imagine you work at this factory in Kisumu. Your manager tells you — Tank B always needs less metal. But they hold exactly the same water. Why?' Allow 10–15 seconds of WAIT TIME after posing the question before learners begin writing. Circulate and read 3–4 predictions silently to identify a range of ideas (surface-area reasoning, shape reasoning, misconceptions about volume). Cold-call 4 learners using name cards: choose one who mentions 'shape', one who mentions 'size', one who mentions 'volume', and one with a misconception. Record each prediction verbatim on the board under the heading 'OUR PREDICTIONS — Lesson 1.' Say: 'We are NOT confirming or correcting these yet. We will return to them at the end of today and at the end of the unit.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Predict-before-instruction (Eliciting Prior Knowledge): By committing predictions to paper before any instruction, learners activate prior knowledge about shape and material, create a cognitive anchor for new content, and generate personal investment in resolving the puzzle. Recording predictions publicly on the board creates accountability and a reference point for revision throughout the unit.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has written at least 3 sentences and a sketch in their book within 4 minutes. Teacher scans books during circulation — look for whether learners distinguish between 'how much water a tank holds' (volume) and 'how much metal is used' (surface area). Flag learners who conflate the two concepts — these learners need targeted scaffolding in the Explain phase. Cold-call responses reveal the range of prior conceptions to inform pacing.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in groups of 4. Each group receives a pre-printed net cut-out kit containing: (a) a net of a rectangular prism (cuboid) labelled with dimensions representing a simple rectangular metal water trough — length 80 cm, width 40 cm, height 40 cm; (b) a net of a triangular prism representing a triangular roof support beam used in tank assembly — triangular face base 30 cm, height 20 cm, length 60 cm; (c) a net of a square-based pyramid representing a decorative roof cap — base 40 cm × 40 cm, slant height 35 cm. Each group also has a plain A3 sheet, ruler, and scissors. Task 1 (10 min): Learners fold each net into its 3D solid and observe: 'What faces does each solid have? How many? What shape are they?' They complete an observation table in their books: Solid | Number of faces | Shapes of faces | Sketch of net. Task 2 (8 min): Learners unfold each solid back to its net and, WITHOUT any formula, physically count and measure the area of each face by subdividing into known shapes (rectangles, triangles), then sum all face areas to estimate total surface area. They record their totals.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute net kits to groups before the phase begins to save time. Give clear verbal instructions: 'Step 1 — fold each net. Step 2 — observe the faces. Step 3 — unfold and calculate total area face by face. Do NOT use a formula yet — use only what you know about rectangles and triangles.' Set a visible timer on the board for each task. Circulate actively every 90 seconds. Ask probing questions to groups: 'How many rectangular faces does your prism have?', 'Are any faces identical? How does that help you?', 'If this net were a real sheet of metal at the Kisumu factory, what would cutting along these lines mean?' After Task 2, ask one group to present their rectangular prism working on the board. Allow 10–15 seconds of WAIT TIME before cold-calling if no group volunteers. Cold-call 2 groups to compare totals — prompt: 'Did both groups get the same answer? If not, what might have caused the difference?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Net Construction and Face Inventory (Concrete-to-Abstract Bridge): Physically folding and unfolding nets connects the abstract idea of 'total surface area' to the tangible factory action of cutting and folding metal sheets. Summing face areas before seeing a formula builds procedural understanding from first principles, ensuring the formula is later recognised as a shortcut rather than a mystery rule.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Each group's observation table is complete with correct face counts (cuboid = 6, triangular prism = 5, square pyramid = 5). (2) Group surface area totals for the rectangular prism are within 5% of the correct value: 2(80×40 + 80×40 + 40×40) = 2(3200+3200+1600) = 16,000 cm². (3) Teacher checks that learners are summing ALL faces (a common error is omitting the base). Prompt any group missing the base: 'In the factory, does the bottom of the tank need metal sheet too?'

framework.learnerExperience: The class reconvenes. The teacher guides learners to generalise their face-counting into formal surface area formulae through structured questioning, using the factory context throughout. Part A — Rectangular Prism (Cuboid): Learners are guided to express: SA = 2(lw + lh + wh). They apply this to find the metal needed to make an open-topped rectangular water storage trough at the Kisumu factory (open top means subtract one lw face). Part B — Triangular Prism: Learners derive SA = 2(½ × b × h) + (perimeter of triangle × length) = bh + (a + b + c)l, connecting each term to a named face of the net. Part C — Square-Based Pyramid: Learners derive SA = l² + 4 × (½ × l × s) = l² + 2ls, where l = base side, s = slant height. They apply this to find the metal needed for a decorative pyramid roof cap (base side 40 cm, slant height 35 cm). Learners then revisit their Predict Phase predictions and individually annotate: 'I now know that surface area means… My original prediction was… I would now change/keep it because…'
framework.teacherMoves: Use the board to build each formula incrementally — write each face's area expression, then combine. Use colour-coding: rectangular faces in blue, triangular faces in red, square base in green. Ask at each step: 'Which face of the net does this term represent? Point to it on your unfolded net.' Allow 10–15 seconds WAIT TIME. Cold-call 3 learners per formula derivation. Explicitly connect each formula to the factory context: 'If you are a metal sheet cutter at the Kisumu factory, this term — 2lw — tells you what?' After the pyramid formula, pose: 'The factory makes 200 pyramid roof caps per week. Each one needs how many square centimetres of metal sheet? How many square metres is that?' (Answer: 200 × (40² + 2×40×35) = 200 × (1600+2800) = 200 × 4400 = 880,000 cm² = 88 m².) Give 3 minutes for individual working, then cold-call 2 learners to present. For the prediction-annotation task, give 3 minutes silent individual writing. Say: 'Write in your own words — no group discussion yet.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Incremental Formula Derivation with Contextual Anchoring: Each algebraic term in the formula is traced back to a physical face of the factory metal sheet. This prevents rote memorisation and builds transferable understanding. The prediction-annotation task (I used to think… now I think…) makes conceptual change visible and metacognitive, reinforcing that science and mathematics learning is a process of refining ideas with evidence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Learners can correctly identify which term in each formula corresponds to which face — check by asking 3 cold-called learners to point to the net face as they name the term. (2) The pyramid roof cap problem is solved correctly by at least 80% of learners working individually (teacher circulates and checks books). (3) Prediction annotations show movement from vague 'shape-based' reasoning toward precise 'surface area formula' reasoning. Flag learners whose annotations still conflate surface area and volume — provide a direct prompt: 'Surface area is about the outside skin. Volume is about what fits inside. Are they the same?'

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives two sticky notes (or writes in a designated DQB section of their book if physical sticky notes are unavailable). On sticky note 1 (WHAT WE KNOW — green): learners write one thing they are now confident about from today's lesson connected to the main driving question. On sticky note 2 (WHAT WE STILL WONDER — yellow): learners write one question that today's lesson raised or left unanswered about the Kisumu tank phenomenon. Learners post their notes on the class Driving Question Board (a section of the classroom wall or a large sheet of manila paper headed: 'HOW DOES SHAPE DETERMINE HOW MUCH MATERIAL IS NEEDED AND HOW MUCH A SOLID CAN HOLD?'). The teacher reads 5–6 posted questions aloud and clusters them into emerging themes: (a) questions about cylinders/hemispheres, (b) questions about frustums, (c) questions about volume vs. surface area relationships, (d) questions about cost. The teacher says: 'These are our investigation questions for the unit. Each lesson will help us answer more of them.'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes or direct learners to a dedicated page in their books. Give exactly 3 minutes for silent writing. Circulate to ensure all learners write a genuine question on their yellow note — prompt any learner who is stuck: 'Think about Tank A and Tank B. What do you still not understand about why they use different amounts of metal?' Read selected questions aloud with enthusiasm: 'Ooh — someone is asking whether a sphere would use even LESS metal for 5,000 litres. That is a fantastic question — we will get to that!' Physically cluster sticky notes into themes on the board using a marker to draw circles around clusters and label them. Photograph the DQB for the class record. Say: 'This board will grow every lesson. By the end of this sub-strand, we will have answered every single cluster.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board — Know/Wonder Protocol: The DQB externalises and organises the class's collective growing understanding. Separating 'know' from 'wonder' makes epistemic progress visible over time and gives learners agency over the direction of investigation. Clustering questions teaches learners to see conceptual relationships between sub-questions, mirroring how professional engineers and scientists organise complex problems.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every learner has posted or recorded both a 'know' and a 'wonder' entry. (2) 'Know' entries reference surface area vocabulary (net, face, formula, prism, pyramid) rather than vague statements. (3) 'Wonder' entries show genuine engagement with the phenomenon — questions about cylinders, frustums, volume, cost, or material efficiency indicate readiness for upcoming lessons. (4) Teacher notes which concept clusters attract the most 'wonder' questions — this informs differentiation and pacing in Lessons 2–5.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners individually draw the first version of their Cumulative Unit Model in their books. The model is a structured diagram with three columns: SHAPE | NET (sketch) | SURFACE AREA FORMULA. Today they complete rows for: (1) Rectangular Prism (Cuboid), (2) Triangular Prism, (3) Square-Based Pyramid. Under each shape sketch, learners write one sentence connecting it to the Kisumu factory context — for example: 'A rectangular prism net is like a flat metal sheet cut at the factory that, when folded, forms a box-shaped water trough.' At the bottom of the model page, learners write: 'UNRESOLVED: We do not yet know the surface area of the cylindrical or frustum tanks in Kisumu — that is what we are investigating.' The teacher reminds the class: 'This model will grow every lesson. Do not throw it away — you will add to it in Lessons 2, 3, 4, and 5.'
framework.teacherMoves: Draw the three-column model template on the board before the phase begins. Give learners 6 minutes of silent individual work. Circulate and check that all three rows are complete and that sentences are specific (not just 'it is used in factories'). Prompt learners who write generic sentences: 'Which specific product at the Kisumu factory could this shape represent? How many cm² of metal does it need?' Cold-call 2 learners to read their contextual sentences aloud. Close the lesson by returning to the original Predict Phase predictions still written on the board: 'Look at your prediction from the start of class. Has today's lesson changed your thinking? Thumbs up if yes, thumbs sideways if partly, thumbs down if your thinking has not changed.' Do a quick visual count and say: 'By the end of this unit, I predict all thumbs will be pointing firmly up — because we will have fully solved the Kisumu tank mystery.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Building (Progressive Schematisation): The growing model functions as a personalised reference tool and a visible record of conceptual growth. Requiring a contextual sentence per shape prevents the model from becoming a passive formula list and reinforces the driving question connection. The 'UNRESOLVED' section at the bottom maintains productive tension and intellectual curiosity, ensuring learners see today's lesson as a starting point rather than a conclusion.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) All three model rows are complete with a sketch, correct formula, and a Kenya-relevant contextual sentence. (2) The 'UNRESOLVED' section is written in the learner's own words and names at least one shape from the phenomenon (cylinder, hemisphere, or frustum). (3) The thumbs survey gives the teacher a rapid whole-class confidence gauge — learners with 'thumbs down' should be identified for follow-up at the start of Lesson 2.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Did learners' predictions in Phase 1 reveal a clear distinction between surface area and volume, or did the majority conflate the two concepts? If conflation was widespread, plan a brief 5-minute clarification opener for Lesson 2 using a real Kenyan example — e.g., 'wrapping a mandazi box uses surface area; filling the box with mandazis uses volume.' (2) During the net activity (Phase 2), did any groups fail to include all faces when summing areas? Identify the specific face most commonly omitted (typically the base) and address this explicitly in the Lesson 2 warm-up. (3) Were learners able to derive the formulae independently through guided questioning, or did they require the teacher to provide the formula directly? If the latter, consider introducing a structured 'formula derivation scaffold' card for lower-attaining groups in Lesson 2. (4) Review the DQB sticky notes: Do the 'wonder' questions suggest learners are genuinely curious about cylinders and frustums (indicating readiness for Lesson 3), or are most questions still about prisms and pyramids (indicating the need for consolidation in Lesson 2)? (5) Did the Kenyan factory context feel authentic and engaging to learners, or did it feel contrived? Note 2–3 specific learner comments or questions that demonstrate genuine connection to the phenomenon for use in future lesson planning.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners folded and unfolded pre-printed nets of a rectangular prism (a water trough), a triangular prism (a roof support beam), and a square-based pyramid (a decorative roof cap) — all modelled as flat metal sheets from the Kisumu factory. They measured and summed individual face areas to estimate total surface area before seeing any formula.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners derived and applied the surface area formulae for rectangular prisms (SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)), triangular prisms (SA = bh + (a+b+c)l), and square-based pyramids (SA = l² + 2ls). They connected each algebraic term to a specific face of the net, and applied formulae to calculate the metal sheet needed for factory products.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The total surface area of a prism or pyramid is the sum of the areas of all its faces, which can be found by unfolding the solid into a flat net. Different solid shapes — even if they enclose the same volume — can have very different surface areas. This is why the frustum tank in Kisumu uses less metal sheet than the cylinder-hemisphere tank despite holding the same 5,000 litres of water.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: surface area, prisms, pyramids, nets, rectangular prism, triangular prism, square-based pyramid, formula derivation, volume, Kisumu, water tank, metal sheet, manufacturing, Kenya, Grade 10, CBE, solid shapes, face area, frustum, cylinder, phenomenon

LESSONS.title: Surface Area of Cones and Spheres
LESSONS.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.4: Surface Area and Volume of Solids

slo.purpose: To extend learners' understanding of surface area from flat-faced solids (Lesson 1) to curved solids — specifically cones and spheres — by deriving their surface area formulae and applying them to real Kenyan manufacturing and architectural contexts.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) identify the components of the surface area of a cone (base circle and lateral/curved surface); (b) derive the formula for the lateral surface area and total surface area of a cone (SA = πr(r + l), where l is the slant height); (c) state and apply the formula for the surface area of a sphere (SA = 4πr²); (d) calculate the surface area of cones and spheres in real-world contexts.
slo.skills: Deriving geometric formulae from first principles; applying surface area formulae accurately; converting units; interpreting net diagrams of curved solids; problem-solving using cone and sphere surface area.
slo.attitudes: Curiosity about why curved surfaces require different formulae from flat-faced solids; appreciation of how geometry underpins cost-efficient manufacturing decisions by Kenyan engineers and fabricators.
slo.keyInquiry: How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 1, learners computed the surface area of prisms and cylinders and began building their DQB around the anchoring phenomenon. In Lesson 2, they extend their evidence-gathering to curved solids — cones and spheres. By examining a conical roof turret found on Nairobi buildings and a spherical mandazi, learners derive the cone and sphere surface area formulae and add new evidence to their DQB, moving closer to explaining why the frustum tank at the Kisumu factory uses less metal than the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. If learners are cutting paper nets, ensure scissors are handled safely and responsibly. Remind learners to handle any physical models (oranges, conical party hats) carefully to avoid injury.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by predicting how the surface area of a cone compares to that of a cylinder of the same height and base radius, connecting back to the Kisumu tank phenomenon. They then observe a physical/paper net of a cone to derive the lateral surface area formula (πrl) and total surface area formula (πr(r + l)), followed by a guided investigation using an orange peel to intuit the sphere formula (4πr²). In the Explain phase, learners solve structured problems set in Kenyan contexts — including calculating the metal sheeting needed for a conical roof turret in Nairobi and the material needed to coat a spherical mandazi mould. The DQB is updated with new evidence about curved surfaces. Learners close the lesson by revising their cumulative solid model to include cones and spheres, annotating it with the new formulae and connections to the tank phenomenon.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown two images side by side on the board: (1) a cylindrical water tank (as discussed in Lesson 1) and (2) a conical roof turret from a building in Nairobi's Westlands. Both have the same base radius (r = 0.7 m) and the same vertical height (h = 1.0 m). In pairs, learners write a prediction in their exercise books: 'Which solid do you think has the greater total surface area — the cylinder or the cone? Give a reason.' Learners also predict whether a sphere would have more or less surface area than both. After 3 minutes, pairs share predictions with the class. Teacher records class predictions as a tally on the board (Cone > Cylinder / Cylinder > Cone / Same) for later verification.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the two images prominently. Say: 'Before we calculate anything today, I want you to think like an engineer at a Nairobi construction firm. Look at these two shapes — same base, same height. Which one do you think needs more material to cover its surface? Write your prediction and a reason in your book. You have 3 minutes — go.' [Allow 3 minutes of silent individual think time.] After 2 minutes, say: 'Now share your prediction with your partner. Do you agree? You have 1 minute.' After sharing, cold-call 4 pairs (ensure gender balance): 'Pair 3 — what did you predict and why?' Record all predictions as a tally. Do NOT confirm or deny. Say: 'Hold onto your predictions — our investigation today will tell us who is right. Let us see what the mathematics says.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction Tally (Activate-and-Confront): Recording class predictions publicly creates cognitive investment. When learners later discover the correct answer, the contrast between prediction and evidence deepens conceptual retention. This strategy also surfaces common misconceptions (e.g., 'taller means more surface area') that the teacher can address explicitly in the Explain phase.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has written a prediction with a stated reason in their exercise book before discussion begins. Teacher circulates during the 3-minute window and scans books. Look for misconceptions such as 'cone has more area because it is pointy' or 'they are the same because same height and radius' — note these to address in Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: PART A — CONE NET INVESTIGATION (30 min): Each group of 4 receives a pre-cut cone net (a circle and a sector of a larger circle) and a complete cone model. Groups are guided through the following steps: (1) Identify the two components of the cone's surface — the circular base (radius r) and the curved lateral surface (sector). (2) Measure the slant height l (the radius of the sector) using a ruler. (3) Notice that the arc length of the sector equals the circumference of the base circle (2πr). (4) Using the formula for the area of a sector (Area = ½ × arc length × radius), derive that Lateral Surface Area = ½ × 2πr × l = πrl. (5) Write the total surface area: SA = πrl + πr² = πr(r + l). Groups record each step in a structured observation table provided on a worksheet. PART B — SPHERE INTUITION ACTIVITY (15 min): Each group receives an orange. They peel the orange carefully and try to flatten the peel onto four circles drawn on paper, each with the same radius as the orange's cross-section. Observation: the peel covers almost exactly 4 circles. Teacher confirms: Surface Area of sphere = 4πr². Learners record this in their notes alongside a labelled diagram.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute cone nets and models. Say: 'Engineers and architects in Nairobi use cone-shaped turrets on buildings. Before they can order metal sheeting, they must know exactly how much curved surface that cone has. Your job right now is to figure out the formula from the net — not from memory.' Walk groups through Step 1: 'Lay your net flat. How many pieces do you see? What shape is each piece?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Cold-call 2 groups. For Step 3, ask: 'What do you notice about the curved edge of the sector and the circle it must attach to?' [WAIT 15 seconds.] Cold-call 3 groups. If groups are stuck on the sector area link, prompt: 'We said arc length of the sector equals 2πr. The sector area formula is ½ × arc length × radius of sector. What is the radius of the sector on your net?' For the orange activity, say: 'Peel your orange carefully and try to lay the peel flat on the four circles. What do you notice?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Cold-call 2 groups. Say: 'This is not a coincidence — Archimedes proved this over 2,000 years ago, and it holds for every sphere. Write the formula: SA = 4πr².' Emphasise: 'No flat base — a sphere is all curved surface.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Net-to-Formula Derivation (Guided Inquiry): Rather than stating formulas, learners reconstruct them from the physical net. This builds formula ownership and reduces dependence on rote memorisation. The orange peel activity uses a concrete, Kenya-familiar object (oranges are grown in Kisii and Meru) to make the abstract 4πr² formula tangible and memorable. Both strategies tie back to the tank phenomenon: understanding where formulae come from helps learners evaluate which tank shape is more material-efficient.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each group's observation worksheet shows a complete, correctly labelled derivation pathway — from sector area to πrl to πr(r + l). Teacher checks 3–4 worksheets during the activity. Key checkpoint question (ask each group): 'Why is l the slant height and not the vertical height in the formula?' Correct answer: because l is the actual length along the curved surface of the cone, not the perpendicular height. Groups unable to articulate this receive a targeted prompt before Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners apply their newly derived formulae to three structured problems set in Kenyan contexts, working individually then comparing with a partner: PROBLEM 1 (Cone): A craftsman in Nairobi is fabricating a decorative conical metal turret for a rooftop. The cone has base radius r = 0.7 m and slant height l = 1.22 m. Calculate the total surface area of the turret to determine how much metal sheet is needed. PROBLEM 2 (Sphere): A bakery in Kisumu uses a spherical mould to shape special occasion mandazi. The mould has a radius of 6 cm. Calculate the surface area of the mould to determine the amount of coating material required. PROBLEM 3 (Comparison — links to Lesson 1): Compare the total surface area of the Nairobi cone turret (Problem 1) with a cylinder of the same base radius (r = 0.7 m) and vertical height (h = 1.0 m) calculated in Lesson 1. Which requires less material? How does this connect to the Kisumu tank phenomenon? After individual work (12 min), a class discussion resolves Problem 3 and revisits the opening prediction tally.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'You now have the tools. Let us put them to work on real Kenyan problems.' Display all three problems on the board. Say: 'Work individually first — 12 minutes. Show every step: formula, substitution, calculation, units.' Circulate actively. For learners substituting vertical height instead of slant height in Problem 1, ask: 'Which length did you use? Is that the length along the surface of the cone or the height inside it?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] For Problem 3, after individual work, facilitate whole-class discussion: 'Who found that the cone has less surface area than the cylinder? Show us your numbers.' Cold-call 3 individuals to present their working. After Problem 3, return to the prediction tally on the board: 'Look at our predictions from the start of the lesson. What does the mathematics tell us now?' [WAIT 15 seconds.] Cold-call 2 learners to make the explicit link: 'So what does this mean for the frustum tank versus the cylinder-hemisphere tank in Kisumu?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Worked Example to Real-World Transfer (Context-Anchored Practice): Each problem is deliberately embedded in a Kenyan context (Nairobi craftsman, Kisumu bakery) so that formula application feels purposeful rather than abstract. Problem 3 explicitly bridges Lesson 1 and Lesson 2 evidence, building the cumulative argument needed to explain the anchoring phenomenon. Returning to the prediction tally creates a structured 'confirm or revise' moment that consolidates conceptual change.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) In Problem 1, learner correctly identifies l = 1.22 m as slant height and not h = 1.0 m, and obtains SA ≈ 4.22 m² (Accept 4.20–4.24 m²). (2) In Problem 2, learner applies SA = 4πr² with r = 0.06 m (or r = 6 cm consistently) and obtains SA ≈ 452.4 cm². (3) In Problem 3, learner makes an explicit written statement connecting the smaller surface area of the cone to the tank phenomenon. Teacher collects 6 exercise books (random sample) at the end of this phase for a quick review before the next lesson.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives two sticky notes (or writes in their DQB section of their exercise book if physical notes are unavailable). On the first note (EVIDENCE colour): they write one piece of evidence gathered today — e.g., 'A cone with the same base and height as a cylinder has less total surface area.' On the second note (NEW QUESTION colour): they write one new question sparked by today's lesson — e.g., 'What about a shape that is part cone and part something else — like the frustum?' Learners post their notes on the class DQB (or share verbally). Teacher reads 3–4 evidence notes and 3–4 new questions aloud, explicitly linking the new questions to the frustum and composite tank shapes that will be investigated in upcoming lessons.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Our Driving Question asks how shape determines how much material is needed. Look at our DQB from Lesson 1. We added evidence about cylinders and prisms. What new evidence can we add today — about cones and spheres?' Distribute sticky notes (or direct learners to their DQB pages). Say: 'Two sticky notes — one EVIDENCE, one NEW QUESTION. You have 3 minutes.' After 3 minutes, collect and post notes. Read 3 evidence notes aloud and ask the class: 'Is this good evidence for answering our Driving Question? Why?' [WAIT 10 seconds each.] Read 3 new question notes. Say: 'I can see some of you are already asking about the frustum — the exact shape of the second tank in Kisumu. That question is coming very soon in our sequence. Keep it on the board — we will answer it.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board — Evidence Accumulation (Iterative Sense-Making): The DQB makes the progression of the storyline visible. By physically adding evidence from each lesson, learners see their understanding growing toward a full explanation of the Kisumu phenomenon. New question notes ensure the DQB drives forward momentum rather than simply summarising past learning. This metacognitive practice develops the 'Learning to Learn' core competency.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has written at least one evidence statement that correctly references a formula or comparison from today's lesson (e.g., mentions SA = πr(r + l) or SA = 4πr², or a correct numerical comparison). Teacher scans notes during posting. If a learner's evidence note is vague or incorrect (e.g., 'cones are curved'), teacher asks a targeted clarifying question: 'What did you calculate today that proves cones use less material?'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the cumulative 'Surface Area Model' they began in Lesson 1 — a visual reference page in their exercise books (or a group poster) that maps all solids studied so far with their surface area formulae, diagrams, and Kenyan context connections. In Lesson 2, learners ADD to this model: (1) A labelled diagram of a cone showing r, h, and l, with the formula SA = πr(r + l) and an annotation linking l to the Nairobi turret context. (2) A labelled diagram of a sphere showing r, with the formula SA = 4πr² and an annotation linking it to the Kisumu bakery mould context. (3) An updated 'Comparison Table' column showing that for equal base radius and height, Cone SA < Cylinder SA — with a written sentence: 'This helps explain why differently shaped tanks can have different surface areas even if designed for the same purpose.' Learners who finish early begin sketching what a frustum (truncated cone) might look like and hypothesise what its surface area formula might involve.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Open your Surface Area Model from Lesson 1. Today we are adding two new solids — the cone and the sphere. Add labelled diagrams, formulae, Kenyan context, and update your comparison table. You have 8 minutes.' Circulate and look for: (a) correct labelling of slant height l vs vertical height h on the cone diagram; (b) no flat base drawn on the sphere diagram; (c) a correctly written comparison sentence. For early finishers: 'Can you sketch a frustum? It is like a cone with the top cut off. What parts would you need to find its surface area?' [WAIT 15 seconds before assisting.] In the final 2 minutes, cold-call 2 learners to share their updated model with the class. Close by saying: 'Your model is growing lesson by lesson. By the end of this unit, it will give you a complete toolkit to fully explain — and calculate — what is happening at that factory in Kisumu.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision (Progressive Knowledge Building): The running model page makes the growth of mathematical knowledge concrete and personal. Adding each new solid alongside its Kenyan context and a comparison note means learners are not just collecting formulae — they are building a coherent argument. The frustum sketch task for early finishers previews upcoming lessons and sustains intellectual momentum, directly advancing the storyline toward the anchoring phenomenon.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: The updated model page contains (1) a correctly labelled cone diagram with l clearly distinguished from h, (2) the correct formulae for both cone and sphere, (3) at least one Kenyan context annotation, and (4) an updated comparison statement. Teacher conducts a 'model gallery walk' for 2 minutes — examining 5–6 books — before dismissal. Any model missing the l vs h distinction is flagged for follow-up at the start of Lesson 3.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Did the majority of learners correctly distinguish slant height (l) from vertical height (h) in the cone surface area formula? If not, plan a targeted 5-minute recap at the start of Lesson 3 using a physical cone model. (2) Were learners able to make an explicit verbal or written connection between the cone-vs-cylinder surface area comparison (Phase 3, Problem 3) and the Kisumu tank phenomenon? If the link was weak, revisit the phenomenon image at the start of Lesson 3 before proceeding. (3) How many new questions on the DQB mentioned the frustum or 'cut cone'? This signals readiness for Lesson 4 (frustum surface area). (4) Did the orange peel activity successfully build intuition for SA = 4πr²? If learners struggled with the connection, consider using a digital simulation (e.g., GeoGebra sphere net) in Lesson 2's revision or as a homework extension. (5) Review the 6 sampled exercise books: are learners showing correct unit usage (cm², m²)? Unit errors are a persistent source of calculation mistakes in KCSE examinations and should be addressed immediately.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners unfolded a cone net and observed that the curved lateral surface is a sector of a circle. They measured the slant height and identified that the arc length of the sector equals the circumference of the cone's base. They also observed an orange peel laid flat over four circles of equal radius to the orange's cross-section.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: The total surface area of a cone is SA = πr(r + l), where r is the base radius and l is the slant height (not the vertical height). The surface area of a sphere is SA = 4πr², covering the entire curved surface with no flat face. For the same base radius and height, a cone has a smaller total surface area than a cylinder.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The cone surface area formula comes from adding the area of the circular base (πr²) to the lateral surface area derived from the sector (πrl), giving πr(r + l). The sphere formula (4πr²) was derived intuitively from the orange peel activity and confirmed mathematically. These formulae explain why curved solids can enclose or cover space differently from flat-faced solids — a key piece of evidence for understanding why the Kisumu tank manufacturer can use different amounts of metal sheet for tanks of the same volume depending on their shape.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: surface area, cone, sphere, slant height, lateral surface area, curved surface, net diagram, πrl, 4πr², total surface area, cone formula derivation, sphere formula, Nairobi turret, Kisumu bakery, mandazi mould, conical roof, Kenyan manufacturing, solid geometry, Grade 10 Mathematics, CBE Kenya, surface area vs volume, comparison of solids

LESSONS.title: Surface Area of Frustums of a Cone
LESSONS.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.4: Surface Area and Volume of Solids

slo.purpose: To derive and apply the formula for the surface area of a frustum of a cone, connecting it to the design of the frustum-shaped water tank in the anchoring phenomenon.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) identify a frustum as the solid formed when a cone is cut parallel to its base; (b) derive the surface area formula for a frustum of a cone using the slant heights of the original and removed cones; (c) calculate the total surface area of a frustum given its radii and slant height.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) apply the frustum surface area formula to solve real-world problems involving tanks, buckets, and containers; (b) interpret video-based evidence to extract mathematical relationships; (c) construct and annotate a diagram of a frustum showing all key dimensions.
slo.attitudes: The learner appreciates how geometric reasoning enables engineers and manufacturers in Kenya to make cost-effective decisions in designing storage solutions for rural communities.
slo.keyInquiry: How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 1, learners calculated the surface area of the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank. In Lesson 2, they explored how volume is conserved across different shapes. Now in Lesson 3, learners turn their attention to the frustum-shaped tank — the tank that puzzled them by using less metal. By deriving and applying the frustum surface area formula, learners gather the critical piece of evidence needed to explain why the frustum tank is cheaper to manufacture than the composite tank, even though both hold 5,000 litres.
slo.safetyNotes: No physical hazards. Ensure all learners can view the video clearly; adjust screen brightness and volume. Learners with visual impairments should be seated closest to the display. Handle rulers and compasses carefully during diagram construction.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by predicting what happens to a cone's surface when it is cut parallel to its base, annotating a diagram of the Kisumu frustum-shaped tank. They then watch a curated YouTube video on the surface area of a frustum, pausing at key moments to extract the formula derivation. Using a Think-Pair-Share sensemaking strategy, learners derive the formula SA = π(R² + r²) + π(R + r)l and apply it to calculate the surface area of the frustum water tank from the anchoring phenomenon. The lesson concludes with learners updating the Driving Question Board and revising their cumulative solid models to include the frustum, building a direct comparison with the composite tank from Lesson 1.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive a printed outline diagram of a frustum (labelled: 'Frustum-Shaped Tank, Kisumu Factory'). The diagram shows a frustum with dotted lines indicating the removed top cone. Learners individually annotate the diagram, labelling what they believe are the key dimensions needed to find the surface area, and write a prediction: 'I think the formula for the surface area of this frustum will involve… because…'. Learners also write a one-sentence prediction answering: 'Do you think the frustum tank uses more or less metal sheet than the cylinder-hemisphere tank from Lesson 1? Why?' Predictions are written in exercise books before any discussion.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute the frustum diagram handout. Say: 'Look at this diagram of the frustum-shaped tank from the Kisumu factory — the tank that puzzled our Grade 10 visitors. Before we watch any video or do any calculation, I want you to think like an engineer. What measurements would YOU need to find how much metal sheet covers this tank?' Allow 10 seconds WAIT TIME. Say: 'Write your prediction in your book — label the diagram and write your sentence starting with: I think the formula will involve…'. Circulate silently for 4 minutes. Cold-call 3 learners: 'Amara, what did you label?' / 'Odhiambo, what measurements did you choose and why?' / 'Wanjiku, do you agree with the prediction we just heard — why or why not?' Record key prediction terms on the board (e.g., radius, slant height, height). Do NOT confirm or correct — say: 'Excellent thinking. Let's gather evidence to test these predictions.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction Annotation: Learners annotate a contextualised diagram before instruction, activating prior knowledge of cone geometry (from Lesson 2) and creating cognitive dissonance. This strategy surfaces misconceptions (e.g., confusing vertical height with slant height) and gives learners a personal stake in the video evidence they are about to gather.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each learner has labelled at least two dimensions on the frustum diagram and written a complete prediction sentence. Teacher scans books during circulation — look for learners who label only height (missing slant height or radii) as a flag for targeted questioning during the video phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners watch a curated YouTube video on the surface area of a frustum of a cone (approx. 8–10 minutes). The video is paused three times at teacher-designated moments: (1) after the frustum is introduced — learners sketch and label the frustum in their books; (2) after the derivation of the curved surface area formula πl(R + r) — learners write the formula and compare with their prediction; (3) after the complete formula SA = π(R² + r²) + π(R + r)l is stated — learners re-examine their diagram and label slant height l, large radius R, and small radius r. A structured observation guide is projected alongside the video with three sentence stems: 'A frustum is formed by…', 'The slant height l is found using…', 'The total surface area formula is…'.
framework.teacherMoves: Before starting the video say: 'As you watch, fill in the three sentence stems projected on the board. Pause your thinking at each stop — I will freeze the video and ask you to write before we move on.' Play video to Pause Point 1 (~2 min). Freeze. Say: 'Sketch what you see. Label the two circular faces — what are their radii called?' WAIT TIME 10 seconds. Cold-call 2 learners: 'Korir, what did you sketch and label?' / 'Fatuma, how does your diagram compare to your prediction from Phase 1?' Resume. Pause at Point 2 (~5 min). Freeze. Say: 'Write the curved surface area formula — πl(R + r) — in your book. Where have you seen something like this before? Think about the cone formula from our earlier work.' WAIT TIME 12 seconds. Cold-call 1 learner: 'Mutua, how is this related to the cone surface area formula?' Resume. Pause at Point 3 (~8 min). Freeze. Say: 'Copy the full formula. Now look back at your prediction — what did you get right? What surprised you?' WAIT TIME 10 seconds. Cold-call 2 learners.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Note-Taking with Sentence Stems: By providing three sentence stems aligned to the video's key conceptual moments, learners are guided to process the derivation actively rather than passively watching. The three pause points create retrieval practice opportunities and directly test learner predictions, building the habit of comparing prior conceptions against new evidence — a core science and engineering practice.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: At each pause point, learners have written responses in their books. Teacher scans during pauses — specifically check that learners have correctly identified R (large base radius), r (small top radius), and l (slant height of the frustum, NOT the original cone). Learners who write only one radius are flagged for Pair support in Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in pairs using the Think-Pair-Share strategy. Each pair receives a problem card: 'The frustum-shaped tank at the Kisumu factory has a large base radius R = 0.8 m, a small top radius r = 0.4 m, and a slant height l = 1.2 m. (a) Calculate the curved surface area of the frustum. (b) Calculate the area of the two circular faces. (c) Calculate the TOTAL surface area of the frustum tank in m². (d) The factory uses metal sheet that costs Ksh 850 per m². How much does the metal sheet for this tank cost?' Pairs first work independently (5 min), then compare and reconcile answers (5 min), then selected pairs share with the class. Learners then write a 'comparison sentence' in their books: 'The frustum tank has a surface area of ___ m², while the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank from Lesson 1 had a surface area of ___ m². The frustum tank uses ___ m² less/more metal, which saves Ksh ___.' This directly re-engages the anchoring phenomenon.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'This is the moment we have been building toward. You are now going to calculate the actual surface area of the frustum tank from Kisumu — the one that puzzled the Grade 10 visitors.' Distribute problem cards. Say: 'Work independently for 5 minutes first — no talking. Use the formula from your observation notes.' Set a visible 5-minute timer. Circulate — look for unit errors and formula misapplication. After 5 min: 'Now compare with your partner. If your answers differ, work through the steps together to find the disagreement.' After paired work: cold-call 3 pairs to share each sub-answer (a), (b), (c), one pair per sub-part. For (d) ask: 'Naledi, does this cost surprise you? What does this tell the factory manager in Kisumu about which tank is cheaper to build?' WAIT TIME 15 seconds. Consolidate on the board: write the full worked solution using colour-coded steps — red for formula, blue for substitution, green for answer with units. Say: 'Now write your comparison sentence. This is the evidence we needed to start answering our driving question.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with Phenomenon Re-Anchoring: The structured pair work builds procedural fluency, while the comparison sentence forces learners to connect their calculation back to the anchoring phenomenon — the frustum vs. composite tank debate. The cost calculation contextualises the mathematics within a real Kenyan manufacturing decision, making the abstract formula meaningful. This mirrors the NGSS practice of 'Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking' in a real-world engineering context.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each learner's book contains a fully worked solution with correct units (m² for area, Ksh for cost) and a completed comparison sentence. Specific checkpoints: (1) Curved surface area = π × 1.2 × (0.8 + 0.4) ≈ 4.52 m²; (2) Circular faces = π(0.8²) + π(0.4²) ≈ 2.51 m²; (3) Total ≈ 7.03 m²; (4) Cost ≈ Ksh 5,975. Learners whose totals are more than 10% off are identified for small-group follow-up during Phase 5.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board (DQB). Each learner writes on a sticky note (or card): one piece of NEW EVIDENCE gathered today and one NEW QUESTION that arose from today's lesson. Evidence notes are placed in the 'What We Now Know' column; question notes in the 'What We Still Wonder' column. As a class, learners review the board and draw arrows connecting today's evidence (frustum surface area formula) to the driving question: 'How does the shape of a solid determine how much material is needed to build it?'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Look at our Driving Question Board. In Lesson 1 we posted evidence about the composite tank. In Lesson 2 we posted evidence about volume. What new evidence can we post today?' WAIT TIME 10 seconds. Distribute sticky notes. Say: 'Write ONE piece of evidence — something you can now calculate that you could not before — and ONE new question your work today raised. You have 3 minutes.' After writing: invite 4 learners (cold-call) to read their evidence note aloud before posting it. Ask the class: 'Where should this go on the board? Does it help us answer the driving question directly, or does it raise a new sub-question?' Use a red marker to draw arrows from today's evidence notes to the main driving question card. Say: 'We are building a case. Every lesson adds another brick. What shape are we still missing from our investigation?' (Prompt learners to anticipate upcoming lessons on spheres or composite solids.)
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Curation: The DQB is a public, cumulative knowledge-building artefact. By physically placing evidence and questions on the board, learners externalise their thinking and see how each lesson contributes to the overarching investigation. Drawing arrows models scientific reasoning — linking evidence to claims — and helps learners see the frustum lesson not as isolated content but as a chapter in a longer evidentiary story.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has posted at least one evidence note and one question note. Teacher reads notes during posting to identify misconceptions (e.g., a learner who writes 'frustums have more surface area than cylinders' as a general rule — this overgeneralisation is addressed verbally: 'Interesting — is that always true, or only for these specific dimensions?'). The quality of new questions also reveals depth of understanding.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative 'Solid Shape Model' — a growing annotated diagram begun in Lesson 1 that now shows the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank. Today, learners add a side-by-side diagram of the frustum tank, labelled with R, r, l, and the surface area formula. Beneath both diagrams, learners write a 'Designer's Note' in the voice of a Kisumu tank manufacturer: 'I would choose the frustum shape because… / I would choose the composite shape because…', justifying their choice using the surface area values calculated in Phase 3. Learners who finish early are challenged to sketch a third tank shape (e.g., a pure cylinder) and estimate which of the three shapes would use the least material for the same volume.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Open your cumulative model from Lesson 1. Today we are adding the frustum to our model. Draw the frustum side-by-side with the composite tank and label all key dimensions.' Allow 4 minutes for drawing. Circulate and check that learners label R (large radius), r (small radius), and l (slant height) — NOT vertical height h. After 4 minutes: Say: 'Now write your Designer's Note. You are the factory manager in Kisumu. You have the data — the surface areas, the costs. Which shape do you manufacture and why?' WAIT TIME 12 seconds. Cold-call 2 learners to read their Designer's Note: 'Achieng, what would you manufacture and what is your evidence?' / 'Njoroge, do you agree? Would any situation change your decision?' Close the lesson by saying: 'Next lesson, we will investigate the volume of the frustum — then we will have ALL the evidence we need to fully answer our driving question.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Role-Play Justification: Adding to a running annotated model helps learners see knowledge as cumulative and connected rather than lesson-by-lesson fragments. The Designer's Note strategy uses disciplinary role-play to require learners to argue from evidence — a key NGSS practice ('Engaging in Argument from Evidence') — and grounds the mathematics in the real-world decision-making context of Kenyan manufacturing.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: The frustum diagram in learner books is correctly labelled with R, r, and l (not h), and the surface area formula is written beneath it. The Designer's Note contains at least one specific numerical value (e.g., 'the frustum uses 7.03 m² of metal, saving Ksh X compared to the composite tank') as evidence for the choice. Learners who write only qualitative justifications ('it looks smaller') are prompted: 'Can you add a number to support that?'

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Were learners able to distinguish between the slant height of the frustum (l) and the vertical height (h)? If many confused these, consider adding a dedicated 3-minute mini-activity at the start of Lesson 4 where learners use Pythagoras' theorem to derive l from h, R, and r. (2) Did the video pause points generate genuine engagement, or did learners copy passively? If passive copying was observed, restructure Phase 2 for future delivery by assigning different sentence stems to different table groups, creating a jigsaw-style knowledge share. (3) Were learners able to write a meaningful comparison sentence connecting frustum surface area to the composite tank from Lesson 1? If learners could not recall the Lesson 1 value, consider keeping a running 'Evidence Wall' poster in the classroom that displays key calculated values from each lesson for reference. (4) Did the cost calculation (Ksh 850/m²) generate authentic engagement with the Kenya context? If learners seemed disengaged, invite a local hardware or construction materials supplier price list for the next lesson to ground the numbers further. (5) Review the DQB — do the new questions posted suggest learners are ready to investigate frustum volume in Lesson 4, or do they need more consolidation on surface area first?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners watched a video showing how a frustum is formed by cutting a cone parallel to its base, and observed the step-by-step derivation of the curved surface area formula πl(R + r) and the total surface area formula SA = π(R² + r²) + π(R + r)l, pausing at three key moments to sketch, label, and record the formula.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners learned that a frustum of a cone has two circular faces (radii R and r) and a curved lateral surface, and that its total surface area is calculated using SA = π(R² + r²) + π(R + r)l; they applied this formula to calculate that the Kisumu frustum-shaped water tank has a total surface area of approximately 7.03 m², costing approximately Ksh 5,975 in metal sheet.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners explained why the frustum-shaped tank in the anchoring phenomenon uses less metal sheet than the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank by comparing their calculated surface areas, and justified — in the role of a Kisumu tank manufacturer — which shape they would choose to produce based on cost and material efficiency evidence.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: frustum, cone, surface area, slant height, radius, composite solids, water tank, Kisumu, manufacturing, cost efficiency, CBE Grade 10, mathematics, geometry, Kenya, KICD

LESSONS.title: Surface Area of Frustums of a Pyramid
LESSONS.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.4: Surface Area and Volume of Solids

slo.purpose: To enable learners to derive and apply the formula for the surface area of a frustum of a pyramid in real-world manufacturing and storage contexts.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to derive the formula for the surface area of a frustum of a pyramid and apply it to solve problems.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to calculate the surface area of a frustum of a pyramid accurately, connecting geometric reasoning to practical material-estimation problems.
slo.attitudes: By the end of the lesson, the learner should appreciate how mathematical derivation of surface area formulas empowers engineers and manufacturers in Kenya to design cost-effective storage structures.
slo.keyInquiry: How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 3, learners explored the surface area of cones and frustums of cones. In this lesson, learners extend their understanding to frustums of pyramids — the shape used in traditional grain stores in Kitale and in some locally fabricated metal storage hoppers. By deriving the formula for the lateral and total surface area of a frustum of a pyramid and applying it to contextualised problems, learners build a richer picture of how a solid's shape (not just its volume) determines the amount of material needed to construct it — directly advancing their investigation of the Kisumu tank manufacturer's puzzle.
slo.safetyNotes: No physical hazards anticipated. Ensure rulers and compasses are used carefully when constructing net diagrams. Supervise safe use of digital devices when streaming the YouTube video.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by briefly recalling the frustum-of-cone surface area formula from Lesson 3 and connecting it to the anchoring phenomenon. They then watch a focused YouTube video on the surface area of a frustum of a pyramid, pausing at key moments to annotate diagrams. Working in groups, they derive the formula by 'unfolding' a frustum of a pyramid into its net — a base rectangle/square, a top rectangle/square, and four trapezoidal lateral faces — and generalising to a formula. They apply the formula to a Kenyan context problem (metal sheet needed for a frustum-shaped grain store in Kitale), compare their answers with peers, and update the Driving Question Board with new evidence about the relationship between shape and surface area.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a large image of two structures side by side: (A) a cylindrical grain store with a conical roof in Siaya County, and (B) a frustum-of-pyramid-shaped metallic grain hopper used in Kitale. Both are labelled as holding the same volume of grain. Each learner silently writes a prediction in their exercise book answering: 'Which structure do you think uses more sheet metal to build — and why?' They then share predictions with a shoulder partner (Think-Pair-Share). Selected pairs share with the class; the teacher records key prediction words on the board without affirming or correcting.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the image on the board/projector. Say: 'Look carefully at these two grain stores. Both hold the same volume. In your exercise book, write your prediction — which one uses more metal sheet, and give a mathematical reason.' Allow 10 seconds of silent thinking (WAIT TIME). After writing time (~2 minutes), say: 'Turn to your shoulder partner and share your prediction.' After 90 seconds, cold-call 3 pairs (vary by gender and seating zone). Record key words — e.g., 'more faces', 'slanted sides', 'trapezoidal panels' — on the board under the heading 'Our Predictions'. Say: 'We will return to these predictions at the end of the lesson to see who was right and why.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share — Activates prior knowledge of surface area concepts from Lessons 1–3, surfaces learner misconceptions about the relationship between volume and surface area, and creates cognitive dissonance that motivates the derivation activity to follow.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learners' written predictions reference at least one geometric property (e.g., number of faces, slant height, shape of lateral faces). Teacher circulates and notes learners who write only volume-based reasoning (e.g., 'same volume means same surface area') as these learners need targeted questioning during the Explain phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners watch a curated YouTube video on 'Surface Area of a Frustum of a Pyramid' (approx. 10–12 minutes). Before viewing, each learner receives a structured observation worksheet with three columns: (1) What I notice about the shape/net, (2) Key measurements labelled in the video, (3) Questions I have. The teacher pauses the video at three predetermined moments: (a) when the net of the frustum of a pyramid is first shown, (b) when the formula for lateral face (trapezium) area is introduced, and (c) when the full surface area formula is assembled.
