At each pause, learners annotate their worksheet silently for 30 seconds before the video resumes. After the video, learners compare worksheet notes with a group partner.
framework.teacherMoves: Before playing the video, say: 'As you watch, complete your three-column worksheet. Do not copy every word — focus on the shape of the net, the labels used, and any steps you find surprising or unclear.' Play the video. Pause at moment (a) and say: 'Pause here — look at the net. What shapes do you see? Write them in column 1. You have 30 seconds.' (WAIT TIME: 30 seconds.) Resume. Pause at moment (b) and say: 'The video just showed how the area of one lateral face is calculated. Write the formula for the area of a trapezium in column 2 — you have 30 seconds.' (WAIT TIME: 30 seconds.) Pause at moment (c) and say: 'The full formula is about to be summarised. Before it appears — can you predict it from your notes? Write your prediction.' After the video, cold-call 2 learners to read out one observation each. Say: 'Keep these worksheets — you will need them for the derivation activity next.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Note-Taking with Planned Pauses — Rather than passive viewing, the three-pause protocol forces learners to process and record information in real time. Connecting the net diagram to known shapes (squares, rectangles, trapeziums) bridges from prior knowledge to new formula construction.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learners correctly identify the net of a frustum of a pyramid as comprising two rectangular/square faces and four trapezoidal lateral faces on their worksheet. Teacher scans worksheets during the partner-comparison step; learners who have blank or incorrect net descriptions are grouped with stronger peers during the Explain phase group activity.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in groups of four to derive the surface area formula of a frustum of a pyramid from first principles using a guided-discovery worksheet. The worksheet provides: a labelled diagram of a frustum of a square pyramid (bottom side length a, top side length b, slant height l of each trapezoidal face, vertical height h), and the following scaffolded steps: (1) Identify and name each face of the frustum. (2) Write the area formula for the bottom face. (3) Write the area formula for the top face. (4) Derive the area of one trapezoidal lateral face using Area = ½(a + b) × l. (5) Write the total lateral surface area for all four faces. (6) Assemble the complete surface area formula: SA = a² + b² + 4 × ½(a + b) × l = a² + b² + 2l(a + b). Groups then solve the Kitale contextualised problem: 'A frustum-shaped grain store in Kitale has a square base of side 2.4 m, a square top of side 1.2 m, and a slant height of 1.5 m on each triangular face. Calculate the total sheet metal (in m²) needed to construct the store, including the base but excluding the top opening.' Groups present their derivation steps on mini whiteboards or manila paper for a gallery walk.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute the guided-discovery worksheet. Say: 'In your groups, you are going to derive — not just memorise — the surface area formula for a frustum of a pyramid. Use your observation worksheet from the video as a reference.' Circulate and monitor step 4; if groups struggle with the trapezium area, prompt: 'What is the formula for the area of a trapezium? Which two lengths are the parallel sides here?' (WAIT TIME: 15 seconds before assisting.) After 15 minutes of group work, say: 'Write your final formula on your manila paper and stick it up.' Conduct a 5-minute gallery walk. Cold-call 3 groups (different from those called in Phase 1) to explain their derivation step-by-step. Ask probing questions: 'Why do we multiply by 4?' and 'What changes in this formula if the base is a rectangle, not a square?' After the gallery walk, display the standard formula on the board: SA = a² + b² + 2l(a + b) [square base] and generalise for rectangular base: SA = 2(lw) + 2(lw) + lateral faces. Bring the class back to the anchoring phenomenon: 'Think about the frustum tank in Kisumu. The manufacturer uses a frustum of a pyramid for metal sheet panels — does our new formula help explain why the frustum tank might use less metal than the cylinder-hemisphere tank?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Guided Discovery with Gallery Walk — Learners construct the formula themselves rather than receiving it, deepening procedural and conceptual understanding. The gallery walk creates peer accountability and exposes learners to multiple derivation approaches, reinforcing that there is a logical path from geometric properties to algebraic formula.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each group's manila paper shows a correctly assembled surface area formula with all terms labelled, and the Kitale problem answer is within ±0.1 m² of the correct value (SA = 2.4² + 4 × ½(2.4 + 1.2) × 1.5 = 5.76 + 10.8 = 16.56 m², minus the top opening = 5.76 + 4 × ½(2.4+1.2)×1.5 = 5.76 + 10.8 = 16.56 m² total including base). Teacher notes groups that skip the top-face exclusion as a common error to address in the next lesson.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives two sticky notes (or writes in a designated section of their exercise book if sticky notes are unavailable). On the first sticky note (green), they write one piece of new evidence they now have that helps answer the driving question: 'How does the shape of a solid determine how much material is needed to build it and how much it can hold?' On the second sticky note (orange), they write one new question that today's lesson raised for them. Learners post their notes on the class Driving Question Board under the columns 'New Evidence' and 'New Questions'. The teacher reads 3–4 contributions aloud and connects them explicitly to the lesson sequence: 'In Lesson 3 we found the surface area of a frustum of a cone. Today we added the frustum of a pyramid. We are building a toolkit of formulas. What solid shapes are still missing from our toolkit?'
framework.teacherMoves: Hand out sticky notes. Say: 'Take 3 minutes — silently. Green note: what new evidence do you have today that helps answer our big driving question? Orange note: what new question does today's lesson raise for you?' (WAIT TIME: 3 minutes silent writing.) Ask learners to post notes on the DQB. Read aloud 3 green and 2 orange contributions. For each green contribution, ask the class: 'Do you agree this is evidence? Give a thumbs up or thumbs down.' For orange questions, say: 'Which of these questions do you think we will answer in the next few lessons?' Accept 2–3 student responses. Close by pointing to the anchoring phenomenon image: 'Remember our Kisumu factory. The students visiting the factory noticed the frustum metal tank uses less sheet metal. We now have mathematical tools — for both frustum of cone AND frustum of pyramid — to start calculating and comparing. Next lesson, we investigate the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Update — This metacognitive strategy makes learning progress visible and cumulative. By separating 'evidence' from 'new questions', learners practise distinguishing between what they know and what they still need to investigate, a core science and mathematical reasoning practice.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learners' green sticky notes reference a specific mathematical relationship (e.g., 'the formula SA = a² + b² + 2l(a+b) shows that slant height and base size both affect surface area independently of volume'). Vague responses such as 'I learned about frustums' indicate the learner needs re-engagement with the phenomenon connection during the next lesson's warm-up.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to a cumulative 'Shape-Surface Area-Volume Model' diagram that they began in Lesson 1 — a large annotated table/diagram in their exercise books that tracks, for each solid studied: the solid name, a sketch of its net, the surface area formula, and a Kenyan real-world example. Today, learners add a new row for 'Frustum of a Pyramid', sketching the net, recording the derived formula SA = a² + b² + 2l(a + b) (for square base), and noting the Kitale grain store as the real-world example. They then write a two-sentence 'model statement' connecting today's learning to the anchoring phenomenon: e.g., 'A frustum of a pyramid has a surface area that depends on both the top and bottom base lengths and the slant height of its trapezoidal faces. This means the manufacturer in Kisumu can reduce sheet metal usage by adjusting these dimensions independently of the tank's volume.' Learners share their model statements with a partner for peer feedback before a final individual revision.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Open your cumulative model from Lesson 1. We are adding today's solid. Fill in the row for frustum of a pyramid — net sketch, formula, and Kitale grain store as the example. You have 4 minutes.' (WAIT TIME: 4 minutes.) Then say: 'Now write your two-sentence model statement linking today's learning to the Kisumu factory question. Be specific — use the words slant height, base length, and surface area.' After 2 minutes of writing, say: 'Read your statement to your partner. Partner: does the statement use at least two mathematical terms and connect to the Kisumu factory? Give one piece of feedback.' Cold-call 2 learners to read their revised model statements to the class. Close: 'Our model is growing lesson by lesson. We can now compare surface areas of cones, frustums of cones, frustums of pyramids, and more. In Lesson 5, we will bring in the composite solids — and that is when we can truly start to answer our big question about the Kisumu tanks.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision — The progressive model diagram makes conceptual growth tangible and forces learners to connect each new formula to both the net geometry and a real Kenyan context. The two-sentence model statement requires learners to synthesise procedural knowledge (formula) with conceptual understanding (what variables affect surface area), directly addressing the driving question.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learners' cumulative model row for 'Frustum of a Pyramid' contains a correctly labelled net sketch (square/rectangle top, square/rectangle base, four trapeziums), the correct formula, and a Kenyan real-world example. The two-sentence model statement explicitly names at least two variables (slant height and base length) and references the anchoring phenomenon. Learners who omit the net sketch or who copy the cone formula incorrectly are identified for targeted revision in Lesson 5's warm-up.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) During the guided-discovery group work, which steps in the derivation caused the most confusion — was it identifying the trapezoidal faces, applying the trapezium area formula, or assembling the full expression? What targeted scaffolding will you provide in Lesson 5's warm-up? (2) Review the green sticky notes from the DQB update: do learners' 'evidence statements' show genuine understanding that surface area and volume are independent properties of a solid? If most statements are procedural ('I can use the formula') rather than conceptual ('slant height and base size affect surface area independently of volume'), plan a brief conceptual discussion for the start of Lesson 5. (3) Did all learner groups correctly exclude the top opening in the Kitale grain store problem? If not, this is a persistent real-world application error — consider a short 'what faces do we include?' checklist tool for future lessons. (4) Were there learners who struggled to connect the frustum-of-pyramid formula to the Kisumu anchoring phenomenon during model building? Note their names and ensure they are cold-called early in Lesson 5 to rebuild that connection.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners watched a YouTube video on the surface area of a frustum of a pyramid, pausing to annotate a three-column observation worksheet identifying the net components (two square/rectangular faces and four trapezoidal lateral faces) and the steps used to assemble the surface area formula.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners derived the surface area formula for a frustum of a square pyramid — SA = a² + b² + 2l(a + b) — by decomposing the solid into its net, calculating the area of each face type, and summing them. They applied this formula to calculate the sheet metal required for a frustum-shaped grain store in Kitale.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners explained, using their derived formula and their cumulative solid model, how the slant height and the top and bottom base lengths of a frustum of a pyramid independently determine its surface area — regardless of the volume enclosed — connecting this insight to the anchoring phenomenon of the Kisumu tank manufacturer's choice of shape to minimise material cost.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: frustum of a pyramid, surface area, slant height, trapezoidal face, net diagram, square base, rectangular base, grain store, Kitale, Kisumu tank manufacturer, composite solids, lateral surface area, formula derivation, cumulative model, driving question board

LESSONS.title: Surface Area of Composite Solids
LESSONS.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.4: Surface Area and Volume of Solids

slo.purpose: To extend learners' understanding of surface area by computing the total exposed surface of composite solids — solids formed by combining two or more basic 3D shapes — and to connect this skill directly to understanding why the cylinder-hemisphere water tank from the Kisumu factory requires more metal sheet than the frustum tank, despite holding the same volume.
slo.knowledge: Learners will know that: (a) a composite solid is formed by joining two or more basic solids; (b) when two solids are joined, the faces at the junction are hidden and must be subtracted from the total surface area; (c) the surface area of a composite solid = sum of surface areas of all component solids − 2 × area of each hidden (joined) face; (d) the cylinder-hemisphere tank's surface area can be computed systematically by identifying each exposed region.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, learners should be able to: (a) identify the component solids in a composite solid and label each exposed surface region; (b) determine which faces are hidden at junctions and subtract their areas correctly; (c) calculate the total surface area of composite solids including the cylinder-hemisphere tank; (d) compare the surface area of the cylinder-hemisphere tank with that of the frustum tank (from Lessons 3–4) holding the same volume.
slo.attitudes: Learners will: (a) appreciate that systematic mathematical decomposition of complex shapes leads to accurate and efficient solutions; (b) recognise that the choices engineers in Kenya make about tank shape are grounded in precise mathematical reasoning; (c) show persistence and care when managing multi-step surface area calculations.
slo.keyInquiry: How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume? / How do we apply surface area and volume of solids in day-to-day activities?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 3 and 4, learners calculated the surface area and volume of the frustum-shaped tank. In Lesson 5, they now turn their attention to the other tank at the Kisumu factory — the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank. By computing its surface area and comparing it with the frustum's surface area (same volume, 5,000 L), learners gather critical evidence to answer why the factory's frustum tank uses noticeably less metal sheet. This lesson is the pivot point in the evidence-gathering sequence: learners for the first time have surface area figures for BOTH tanks in front of them.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. Ensure shared calculators and digital devices are handled responsibly. If the YouTube video is played, ensure screen brightness is comfortable and volume is set appropriately for the classroom environment. Learners with visual impairments should be seated near the display screen.

LESSONS.overview: Lesson 5 of 10 in the evidence-gathering sequence for the anchoring phenomenon (Kisumu water tank factory). Learners open by predicting whether the cylinder-hemisphere composite tank will have a larger or smaller surface area than the frustum tank they computed in Lessons 3–4. They then watch a focused YouTube video segment on the systematic strategy for finding the surface area of composite solids (identify components → subtract hidden faces → sum exposed surfaces). Using this strategy, they work through two applications: (1) a Kericho tea canister (cylinder + cone lid) as a scaffolded practice context, and (2) the cylinder-hemisphere water tank from the anchoring phenomenon, using the same volume-equivalent dimensions established in previous lessons. Learners record both surface area results and compare them directly to the frustum surface area, updating the Driving Question Board with new evidence. The lesson closes with a cumulative model revision in which learners annotate a diagram of both tanks showing where material is 'saved' or 'added' by shape choice.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a side-by-side diagram of the two Kisumu factory tanks: (A) the cylinder-hemisphere composite tank and (B) the frustum tank. Both are labelled as holding 5,000 litres. Learners individually write a prediction in their exercise books: 'Which tank do I think has a larger surface area, and why?' They then share their prediction with a shoulder partner using the sentence frame: 'I predict tank ___ has a larger surface area because ___.' Selected pairs share aloud. Predictions are NOT confirmed yet — they are posted on a class Predict Wall (sticky notes or chalkboard column).
framework.teacherMoves: Display the two-tank diagram on the board (drawn or projected). Say: 'Before we calculate anything today, I want your best mathematical guess. Look at both tanks. Both hold exactly 5,000 litres — same volume. Which one do you think needs more metal sheet to build? Write your prediction and your reasoning in your book. You have 2 minutes — go.' [Allow 2 minutes silent individual thinking.] After writing, say: 'Share with your shoulder partner using this frame: I predict tank ___ has a larger surface area because ___.' [Allow 90 seconds.] Cold-call 4 pairs — 2 who predict cylinder-hemisphere is larger, 2 who predict frustum is larger. Record predictions on the board under 'Predict Wall' columns. Say: 'We are going to test every single one of these predictions with mathematics today. Let's gather our evidence.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Predict-then-Investigate (Anchored Prediction): By committing to a written prediction tied to the anchoring phenomenon before instruction, learners activate prior geometric intuition about surface area vs. volume. This creates productive cognitive dissonance that the lesson's evidence will resolve, deepening retention of the composite surface area concept.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each learner has a written prediction with a geometric or mathematical justification (not just a guess). Teacher scans books during pair-share. Red flag: learners who cannot articulate any reason — teacher prompts: 'Think about which tank has more curved surfaces or flat faces exposed.'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners watch a targeted YouTube video segment (recommended: a composite solids surface area tutorial, e.g., from Math with Mr. J or similar, ~8–10 minutes) that demonstrates the three-step strategy: Step 1 — Identify and list all component solids. Step 2 — Identify hidden/joined faces and calculate their areas. Step 3 — Sum all exposed surface areas. During the video, learners complete a structured Video Observation Guide with three columns: Component Solid | Formula Used | Hidden Face? (Yes/No). After the video, learners examine a physical or drawn model of the Kericho tea canister (a cylinder with a shallow cone lid — a familiar composite solid from Kenyan household contexts). In groups of 3, they label a blank diagram of the canister: cylinder lateral surface, cylinder top (hidden — joined to cone base), cone lateral surface (slant face), cylinder base (open or closed depending on design). They are NOT yet calculating — only identifying and labelling surfaces.
framework.teacherMoves: Before playing the video, distribute the Video Observation Guide. Say: 'As you watch, fill in each component solid you see, the formula the presenter uses, and whether that face is hidden at a joint. You will use these notes for your calculations, so be accurate.' Play the video (8–10 minutes). Pause at the moment the presenter subtracts the hidden circular face and say: 'Pause here — why are we subtracting this area? Discuss with your neighbour for 30 seconds.' [Allow 30 seconds.] Restart video. After the video, distribute or display the Kericho tea canister diagram. Say: 'In your groups of three, label every surface of this canister. Use a different colour or code for: exposed surfaces you INCLUDE and hidden surfaces you SUBTRACT. You have 4 minutes.' [Allow 4 minutes.] Cold-call 2 groups to share their labelling on the board. Correct misconceptions immediately, especially around the circular face shared between cylinder top and cone base.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Video Observation + Diagram Annotation: The Video Observation Guide transforms passive video watching into active evidence collection. Diagram annotation of the Kericho canister then bridges from the abstract video example to a Kenya-relevant context, ensuring learners can transfer the strategy before applying it to the phenomenon tanks.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learners' canister diagrams correctly identify the shared circular face (r = radius of cylinder = radius of cone base) as the ONE hidden face, and all other faces as exposed. Teacher checks group diagrams during the 4-minute activity. Red flag: groups that colour the cylinder's flat top as 'exposed' — teacher asks: 'If I glue the cone base onto the cylinder top, can I still see or access that face from outside?'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work through two connected calculations, scaffolded from easier to phenomenon-level: CALCULATION A — Kericho Tea Canister (Cylinder + Cone): Given: cylinder radius r = 5 cm, cylinder height h = 12 cm, cone slant height l = 7 cm, cone base radius = 5 cm (same as cylinder). Learners apply the strategy: (i) Cylinder: full SA = 2πr(r + h) = 2π(5)(5+12) = 170π cm². But cylinder top is hidden, so subtract πr² = 25π cm². Exposed cylinder area = 145π cm². (ii) Cone: full SA = πr(r + l) = π(5)(5+7) = 60π cm². But cone base is hidden, so subtract πr² = 25π cm². Exposed cone area = 35π cm². (iii) Total composite SA = 145π + 35π = 180π ≈ 565.5 cm². CALCULATION B — Cylinder-Hemisphere Water Tank (Phenomenon): Using dimensions established in prior lessons for a 5,000-litre capacity tank: cylinder radius r = 0.8 m, cylinder height h = 2.0 m, hemisphere radius r = 0.8 m. Learners apply the strategy: (i) Cylinder: lateral surface = 2πrh = 2π(0.8)(2.0) = 3.2π m². Cylinder base (bottom, exposed) = πr² = 0.64π m². Cylinder top is hidden (joined to hemisphere flat face). (ii) Hemisphere: curved surface = 2πr² = 2π(0.64) = 1.28π m². Hemisphere flat base is hidden. (iii) Total SA = 3.2π + 0.64π + 1.28π = 5.12π ≈ 16.09 m². Learners then retrieve their frustum surface area from Lesson 4 notes and record both values in a Comparison Table: Tank | Surface Area (m²) | Volume (L). They answer: 'Which tank uses more metal? By how much (in m²)?' in writing.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute the worked-example scaffold sheet for Calculation A. Say: 'Work through the Kericho canister calculation in your groups using the three-step strategy from the video. Show every step — identify, subtract, sum. You have 6 minutes.' [Allow 6 minutes.] After 6 minutes, cold-call one group to present each step on the board (3 different groups for steps 1, 2, 3). Correct any errors before moving to Calculation B. Say: 'Now for the real question — the tank from Kisumu. These are the actual dimensions we have been using since Lesson 1. Use the exact same three-step strategy.' [Allow 8 minutes for group work on Calculation B.] Circulate and prompt with: 'Which face is touching the hemisphere flat side? Is it exposed to the outside?' and 'What does the cylinder's bottom face represent in the real tank — is it exposed?' After 8 minutes, say: 'Look at the number you got for the cylinder-hemisphere tank. Now find your frustum surface area from Lesson 4. Write both in your Comparison Table.' [Allow 2 minutes.] Ask: 'Who is surprised by what they see? Hands up.' Acknowledge responses. Say: 'This is the mathematical evidence the Grade 10 students at the Kisumu factory were looking for. The numbers are in front of us — what do they tell us?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Scaffolded Parallel Calculation + Comparison Table: Learners first build procedural fluency on a low-stakes Kenya context (Kericho canister), then immediately transfer the identical strategy to the phenomenon context (Kisumu tank). The Comparison Table makes the quantitative contrast between the two tanks explicit and visible, directly advancing the driving question with concrete numerical evidence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learners' Calculation B shows three clearly separated rows (cylinder lateral, cylinder base, hemisphere curved) with the hidden face correctly subtracted and a final answer of approximately 16.09 m², compared to their Lesson 4 frustum surface area. Teacher spot-checks at least 5 groups during independent work. Red flag: learners who include the full cylinder SA formula (2πr(r+h)) without subtracting the hidden top — teacher redirects: 'Draw the tank. Put your finger on the flat circle where the hemisphere sits. Can rain or a hand touch that face? No — so it is hidden. Subtract it.'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board. The DQB has two anchor columns: 'What do we KNOW?' and 'What do we still WONDER?' Each group receives two sticky notes. On the first (green — KNOW), they write one evidence statement from today's lesson, e.g., 'The cylinder-hemisphere tank has a surface area of ~16.09 m² while the frustum tank has ~13.2 m² — for the same 5,000-litre volume, the frustum uses less metal.' On the second (yellow — WONDER), they write one new question that today's evidence raises, e.g., 'Is there a shape that would use even less metal for 5,000 litres?' or 'Does the thickness of the metal sheet also affect cost?' Groups post their notes on the DQB. The teacher facilitates a 3-minute gallery read where learners walk past the board silently, then one spokesperson per group shares the most interesting WONDER note.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'The DQB is where we track what the maths is proving to us. Take your two sticky notes. On the green one, write ONE thing today's calculation proved — use actual numbers. On the yellow one, write ONE new question today's evidence made you think of. You have 3 minutes.' [Allow 3 minutes.] After posting, say: 'Walk past the board — silent gallery read for 90 seconds. Read what other groups wrote.' [Allow 90 seconds.] Then: 'I'm going to cold-call one person from three different groups to share the WONDER question their group posted and explain why today's evidence made them ask it.' Cold-call 3 students. After each, ask the class: 'Does anyone's calculation from today help start to answer this question?' [Allow 10-second wait time after each.] Close by saying: 'Our DQB is building a mathematical case. By the end of this unit, we will be able to answer every question on this board using the mathematics we are learning.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Evidence Posting: Requiring learners to express today's findings as an evidence statement with actual numbers (not just 'we learned surface area') forces precise mathematical communication and connects procedural calculation to the conceptual storyline of the phenomenon. The WONDER notes sustain inquiry momentum across the lesson sequence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Green sticky notes contain a specific quantitative comparison (surface area values, difference in m²) rather than a vague summary. Yellow sticky notes pose a genuine mathematical or engineering question rather than a personal comment. Teacher reads all green notes before the next lesson to identify groups whose surface area value is significantly off — these groups receive targeted feedback before Lesson 6.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive their cumulative model diagram — a cross-section drawing of both tanks (cylinder-hemisphere and frustum) that they have been annotating since Lesson 1. In this lesson's revision, they: (a) shade or colour-code each distinct surface region of the cylinder-hemisphere tank (cylinder base, cylinder lateral, hemisphere curved) in three different colours; (b) write the calculated area of each region next to it (e.g., cylinder base: 0.64π m²); (c) draw a double-headed arrow between the two tanks labelled 'Same Volume, Different Surface Area' and annotate the arrow with the numerical difference in surface area; (d) write one sentence at the bottom of their model: 'The cylinder-hemisphere tank requires ___ m² more metal than the frustum tank because ___.' Learners share their completed model annotation with their group for peer feedback using the prompt: 'Is every surface region labelled and calculated? Is the hidden face correctly shown as subtracted?'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute the cumulative model diagrams (pre-drawn cross-section sheets used since Lesson 1). Say: 'Today you are adding the surface area breakdown of the cylinder-hemisphere tank to your model. Use three colours — one for each exposed surface region — and write the area next to each region. Then annotate the double arrow between the tanks. You have 6 minutes.' [Allow 6 minutes.] Circulate and check that learners are writing numerical values, not just labels. After 6 minutes, say: 'Share your model with your group. Use the peer feedback prompt on the board: Is every surface region labelled and calculated? Is the hidden face correctly shown as subtracted? Give your partner 60 seconds of feedback.' [Allow 2 minutes for peer feedback.] Close: 'By the end of Lesson 10, this model will be your complete answer to the driving question. Every number you add today is evidence the Kisumu factory students — and you — can use to explain their observation. Well done.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Annotated Model Revision: The cross-section diagram serves as a running scientific model that grows more complete with each lesson. Colour-coding surface regions and writing numerical values alongside them makes the abstract formula results spatially concrete. The comparative annotation ('Same Volume, Different Surface Area') directly addresses the driving question and reinforces the central phenomenon insight.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learners' model diagrams show three distinctly coloured and numerically labelled surface regions for the cylinder-hemisphere tank, with the shared circular face explicitly marked as 'hidden — subtracted.' The double-headed arrow is annotated with the correct numerical surface area difference. Teacher collects 5–6 models at random at the end of the lesson to review before Lesson 6. Red flag: learners who have the correct total but cannot identify which region contributes which area — this indicates procedural rather than conceptual understanding and will need addressing in Lesson 6's review opener.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Were learners able to correctly identify the hidden face in the cylinder-hemisphere composite without prompting — or did most groups initially include it in the total? This reveals whether the 'subtract the junction' concept was internalised from the video or needs re-teaching. (2) When learners completed the Comparison Table, what was the emotional or intellectual reaction in the room? Did the numerical difference between the two tanks' surface areas generate surprise, curiosity, or disengagement? This affective response is a key signal of whether the phenomenon is genuinely driving inquiry. (3) How accurate were the Lesson 4 frustum surface area values that learners retrieved from their notes? If many groups had incorrect frustum values, the comparison in this lesson was built on faulty evidence — consider opening Lesson 6 with a brief class consensus on the correct frustum surface area. (4) Did the Kericho canister scaffold (Calculation A) successfully bridge learners from the video to the phenomenon tank (Calculation B), or did most groups stall on Calculation B anyway? If so, consider adding one more intermediate practice example in Lesson 6. (5) Review the green sticky notes from the DQB: how many groups wrote specific numerical evidence versus vague statements? This tells you whether your learners are ready to make quantitative arguments about the phenomenon or still need support translating calculations into claims.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners watched a video demonstration of the three-step composite surface area strategy (identify components, subtract hidden faces, sum exposed surfaces) and examined a labelled diagram of a Kericho tea canister (cylinder + cone) to identify which faces are exposed and which are hidden at the joint.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners calculated that the cylinder-hemisphere water tank has a total exposed surface area of approximately 16.09 m² (5.12π m²), which is measurably larger than the frustum tank's surface area computed in Lessons 3–4 — even though both tanks hold exactly 5,000 litres of water. They learned that the composite surface area equals the sum of all component surface areas minus twice the area of each shared (hidden) face at a junction.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners explained — using their Comparison Table and annotated cumulative model — that the frustum tank uses less metal sheet than the cylinder-hemisphere tank because its shape produces a smaller total surface area for the same enclosed volume, providing direct mathematical evidence to answer the driving question about why the Kisumu factory's frustum tank is more material-efficient.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: composite solids, surface area, cylinder, hemisphere, hidden faces, junction subtraction, Kericho tea canister, Kisumu water tank, frustum comparison, three-step strategy, exposed surfaces, cumulative model, driving question board, Grade 10 mathematics, Kenya CBE

LESSONS.title: Volume of Prisms, Pyramids, Cones, and Spheres
LESSONS.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.4: Surface Area and Volume of Solids

slo.purpose: Learners derive and apply formulae for the volume of prisms, pyramids, cones, and spheres, and use these to compare the storage capacities of differently shaped grain stores — advancing the driving question by establishing that volume and surface area are independent properties of a solid.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) state the volume formulae for prisms (including cuboids and cylinders), pyramids, cones, and spheres; (b) derive the relationship V(pyramid) = ⅓ × V(prism) with the same base and height; (c) calculate the volume of composite solids involving combinations of these shapes; (d) compare volumes of differently shaped solids to explain observed differences in storage capacity.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) select and correctly apply the appropriate volume formula for a given solid; (b) perform multi-step volume calculations with correct units (cm³, m³, litres); (c) convert between volume units (1 m³ = 1 000 litres); (d) construct and revise a cumulative class model linking solid shape to volume and surface area.
slo.attitudes: Learners appreciate that mathematical formulae are practical tools used by Kenyan engineers, grain-store designers, and water-tank manufacturers to make cost-effective decisions, and take responsibility for the accuracy of their calculations as a form of professional integrity.
slo.keyInquiry: How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 6, learners shift the investigative lens from surface area (Lessons 3–5) to volume. Using the Kitale maize storage supporting phenomenon, they compute and compare the volumes of a rectangular grain store (prism) and a conical grain store, explaining why a farmer notices that two structures of the same height and base width store different amounts of maize. This finding feeds directly back to the Kisumu tank anchoring phenomenon: both the cylindrical-hemispherical tank and the frustum tank hold 5 000 litres — learners now have the mathematical tools to verify that claim and to understand how manufacturers engineer equal volumes into different shapes.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used. Where physical containers of water or sand are used for demonstration, ensure surfaces are dry to prevent slipping. Handle any glass measuring cylinders with care.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by predicting which of two grain stores — a rectangular prism store and a conical store of identical base and height dimensions — holds more maize, connecting to the Kitale supporting phenomenon. Through guided derivation and a hands-on sand/water displacement activity, they establish volume formulae for prisms, pyramids, cones, and spheres, discovering the ⅓ relationship between cone/pyramid and prism/cylinder volumes. They then calculate, compare, and verify volumes of the two tank designs from the Kisumu anchoring phenomenon, confirming the manufacturer's claim of equal 5 000-litre capacity. The lesson closes with model revision on the class Driving Question Board, where learners articulate that shape affects surface area and volume independently — a key disciplinary insight.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners examine a labelled diagram (projected or printed) showing two grain stores used by a farmer in Kitale, Trans-Nzoia County: Store A is a rectangular prism (length 3 m, width 3 m, height 4 m) and Store B is a cone (base radius 1.5 m, height 4 m). Both stores have the same base width (3 m diameter / 3 m side) and the same height (4 m). Working individually for 3 minutes, learners write a prediction in their exercise books: 'Which store holds more maize — Store A, Store B, or do they hold the same amount? Give a reason for your prediction.' They then share predictions with a shoulder partner (1 minute) before a whole-class show-of-hands poll.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the two grain-store diagrams on the board with all dimensions clearly labelled. Say: 'A farmer in Kitale has built two grain stores — both are the same height and the base of each store fits inside the same 3-metre square footprint. He is puzzled: one store seems to hold much more maize than the other. Before we calculate anything, I want your best scientific prediction.' Circulate silently for 3 minutes — do NOT answer questions about formulae. After the shoulder-partner share, conduct a cold-call poll: call on 4 learners (at least one from each prediction camp — Store A, Store B, same). Ask each: 'What reasoning led you to that prediction?' Apply 10-second wait time before accepting responses. Record tally of predictions on the board (Store A / Store B / Same) and say: 'Let us hold these predictions — our calculations will tell us who was right and why.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction Anchoring — by committing to a written prediction before instruction, learners activate prior intuitions about shape and space. The shoulder-partner share surfaces diverse reasoning (e.g., 'a cone tapers so it must hold less' vs. 'they have the same height so it should be the same'), creating productive cognitive conflict that motivates the derivation activity.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has a written prediction with a stated reason in their exercise book before the poll. Teacher scans books during circulation to check reasoning quality. Key diagnostic: learners who say 'same amount because same height and base' reveal a misconception about how shape affects volume — flag these learners for targeted questioning during Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Activity 2A — Hands-on Derivation (Cone vs. Cylinder): In groups of four, learners receive a cylindrical container and a conical container of identical base radius and height (e.g., a tin cylinder and a paper cone made from the template provided, both with r = 5 cm, h = 10 cm). Groups fill the cone with dry sand, then pour it into the cylinder, repeating until the cylinder is full. Learners record: 'How many full cones of sand fill the cylinder?' They record the result (3 cones = 1 cylinder) in a results table. Activity 2B — Formula Derivation Reading: Learners read a structured half-page text (provided as a printed card or displayed on screen) that presents the volume formulae for a prism (V = Base Area × height), pyramid (V = ⅓ × Base Area × height), cylinder (V = πr²h), cone (V = ⅓πr²h), and sphere (V = 4/3 πr³), with a worked example for each using Kenyan contexts (a cylindrical water tank in Kisumu, a conical grain store in Kitale, a spherical gas container in Nairobi). Activity 2C — Worked Calculation Pair Task: Pairs work through two calculations: (1) Volume of Store A (rectangular prism, 3 × 3 × 4 m); (2) Volume of Store B (cone, r = 1.5 m, h = 4 m). They record answers in m³ and convert to litres.
framework.teacherMoves: For Activity 2A: Distribute materials and say: 'Your task is not to guess — measure. Fill the cone, pour it into the cylinder, count how many times. Record the number accurately.' Monitor all groups; after 5 minutes, ask: 'Groups, what number did you get?' Cold-call 3 groups. Write results on the board. Say: 'What mathematical relationship does this suggest between the volume of a cone and the volume of a cylinder with the same base and height?' Wait 12 seconds. Cold-call 2 learners. Confirm: 'Yes — the cone holds exactly one-third of the cylinder. This is not a coincidence — it is a geometric truth we can prove with calculus later in your schooling, but today we will use it as our working rule.' For Activity 2B: Say: 'Read the formula card carefully. As you read, underline the ⅓ factor wherever it appears and ask yourself: which solids share this factor and why?' For Activity 2C: Circulate during pair work. When a pair finishes, ask: 'How did you convert your answer from m³ to litres?' (Target answer: multiply by 1 000.) If a pair struggles with the cone formula, prompt: 'What is the base shape of the cone? What is its area? Now multiply by height and then by…?' Wait 10 seconds.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Concrete-Representational-Abstract (CRA) Progression — learners first handle physical objects (cone and cylinder with sand) to observe the ⅓ relationship concretely, then read its symbolic representation in the formula card, and finally apply it abstractly in calculation. This three-step sequence builds durable conceptual understanding rather than rote formula memorisation, directly aligned with NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 3 (Planning and Carrying Out Investigations) and Practice 5 (Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking).
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every group correctly records '3 cones = 1 cylinder' (or equivalent) in their results table — groups recording other values need immediate re-measurement support. (2) In pair calculations, Store A volume = 36 m³ = 36 000 litres; Store B volume = ⅓ × π × 1.5² × 4 ≈ 9.42 m³ ≈ 9 420 litres. Teacher checks at least 6 pairs' working during circulation. Flag any pair that omits the ⅓ factor or forgets to square the radius.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to their Phase 1 predictions and self-assess in writing: 'Was your prediction correct? What does the calculation tell you about how shape affects volume?' (2 minutes individual writing.) Groups of four then tackle the Explain Challenge — a three-part problem printed on a task card: Part 1: A farmer in Kitale claims his rectangular grain store (3 m × 3 m × 4 m) holds nearly 4 times more maize than his neighbour's conical store (r = 1.5 m, h = 4 m). Use your calculations from Phase 2 to verify or refute this claim and express the ratio of their volumes. Part 2: A spherical metal gas container in a Nairobi industrial park has a radius of 2 m. Calculate its volume in m³. Part 3 (Extension — connects back to anchoring phenomenon): The Kisumu tank manufacturer claims both tanks hold exactly 5 000 litres. The cylindrical-hemispherical tank has a cylinder of radius 0.7 m and height 2.8 m, topped with a hemisphere of radius 0.7 m. Calculate the total volume of this composite tank in litres and assess whether the manufacturer's claim is accurate. Groups record full working and a written conclusion sentence. A spokesperson from each group shares one part of the solution in a class gallery walk.
framework.teacherMoves: Open Phase 3 by returning to the prediction tally on the board: 'Let us see what our evidence says.' Cold-call one learner from each prediction group to read their self-assessment. Affirm accurate reasoning and gently correct misconceptions: 'Store B is a cone — it tapers to a point, so even though it has the same base and height, it only fills one-third of the space a cylinder would fill. That is why shape matters so much.' Distribute the Explain Challenge task cards. For Part 3, scaffold with: 'What two shapes make up the composite tank? Write the formula for each, then add them.' During the gallery walk, pose probing questions to each group: 'What does your answer in litres tell you about the manufacturer's claim?' 'If your answer is not exactly 5 000 litres, what might explain the difference?' Apply 12-second wait time consistently. After the gallery walk, deliver a 5-minute direct teaching summary on the board, writing all four formulae clearly with their Kenyan context examples, and explicitly stating: 'Volume tells us capacity — how much a solid can hold. Surface area tells us material — how much it costs to build. These two properties are independent of each other — that is the central mathematical truth of this entire unit.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) Writing — learners write a conclusion sentence for each task part following the structure: Claim (what the answer shows), Evidence (the calculated values), Reasoning (the formula and mathematical principle applied). This strategy, aligned with NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 6 (Constructing Explanations), trains learners to communicate mathematical meaning rather than just produce numerical answers, building the analytical writing skills required for KCSE examination responses.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Part 1 — learners correctly compute the volume ratio: 36 ÷ 9.42 ≈ 3.82, confirming the farmer's claim is approximately correct. Accept answers between 3.7 and 3.9. (2) Part 2 — V(sphere) = 4/3 × π × 2³ ≈ 33.51 m³. (3) Part 3 — V(cylinder) = π × 0.7² × 2.8 ≈ 4.31 m³; V(hemisphere) = 2/3 × π × 0.7³ ≈ 0.718 m³; Total ≈ 5.03 m³ ≈ 5 030 litres — close to 5 000 litres (manufacturer's claim is approximately valid). Teacher uses these expected answers as a diagnostic rubric during the gallery walk.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board displayed at the front of the room. Each group receives two sticky notes of different colours: a GREEN note ('Evidence we now have') and an ORANGE note ('New questions this raises'). Groups write one entry for each colour based on today's lesson findings and post them on the DQB under the relevant driving question column. Individually, learners also update their personal DQB tracker in their exercise books, completing the sentence stems: 'Today I found evidence that… [connect to volume formulae and Kitale phenomenon]' and 'This changes/confirms my thinking about the Kisumu tanks because…'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'We have now investigated surface area across five lessons and today we have explored volume. The Driving Question asks how shape determines both material needed AND how much a solid can hold. What new evidence can we post today?' Give groups 3 minutes to write their sticky notes. As groups post, read 2–3 aloud and ask the class: 'Does everyone agree this counts as evidence? Does it directly address our driving question?' Cold-call 2 learners to read their personal tracker entries. Highlight for the class: 'Notice that today we confirmed the manufacturer's claim — the cylindrical-hemispherical tank does hold approximately 5 000 litres. In the next lesson, we will investigate the frustum tank to see if that claim also holds, and then we can compare their surface areas to explain why one uses less metal.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Protocol — the structured posting of evidence and new questions makes the cumulative nature of the inquiry visible to all learners. The colour-coded system distinguishes what is known (green) from what remains to be investigated (orange), modelling authentic scientific practice (NGSS Practice 1: Asking Questions) and helping learners track the arc of understanding across all 10 lessons.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: Green sticky notes must contain a specific mathematical finding (e.g., 'The cone holds only ¼ of what the rectangular prism holds for the same base and height'). Orange sticky notes must contain a genuine investigative question connected to the phenomenon (e.g., 'Does the frustum tank also hold 5 000 litres? We need to check!'). Teacher reads all posted notes after class to identify misconceptions for addressing in Lesson 7.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners individually revise and extend their cumulative explanatory model — a labelled diagram or annotated sketch they have been building since Lesson 1. Today's revision task: (1) Add a 'Volume' column or panel to their model alongside the existing 'Surface Area' panel. (2) Annotate at least three solids (cylinder, cone, rectangular prism) with both their surface area formula and their volume formula side by side. (3) Draw an arrow labelled 'Independent properties' between the Surface Area panel and the Volume panel to represent today's central insight. (4) Write one sentence at the bottom of the model: 'A manufacturer in Kisumu can engineer equal volumes into different shapes, but the surface area — and therefore the material cost — will differ depending on the shape chosen.' The class shares models in a 3-minute pair comparison before the lesson closes.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Your model is your growing scientific explanation of how shape, surface area, and volume are related. Today you discovered something powerful: volume and surface area are independent. Your model must show this.' Project a partially completed exemplar model on the board (teacher-prepared) showing the structure but with blank formula spaces, so learners see the format without copying content. Circulate during the 7-minute model revision, prompting: 'Does your model show that the cone and cylinder have the same base and height but different volumes?' and 'Have you connected your model back to the Kisumu phenomenon yet?' After the pair share, cold-call 2 learners to read their closing sentence aloud. Close the lesson by saying: 'In Lesson 7, we will calculate the volume of a frustum — the shape of the second Kisumu tank — and then we will have everything we need to fully explain the factory observation that started our entire investigation.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision — iterative model building across lessons mirrors authentic scientific and engineering practice (NGSS Practice 2: Developing and Using Models). By physically adding the volume panel alongside the surface area panel, learners create a visual representation of the independence of these two properties, making the abstract mathematical relationship cognitively tangible and retrievable.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) The model contains correctly labelled volume formulae for at least three solids. (2) The 'Independent properties' arrow is present and the learner can verbally explain what it means if cold-called. (3) The closing sentence accurately connects the mathematical finding to the Kisumu anchoring phenomenon. Teacher collects 5 randomly selected models at the end of class to assess depth of understanding before Lesson 7.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After the lesson, reflect on the following questions:
1. PREDICTION ACCURACY: What proportion of learners correctly predicted that Store A (rectangular prism) holds more maize than Store B (cone)? What reasoning patterns did incorrect predictors use — and how will you address these in Lesson 7's opener?
2. HANDS-ON DERIVATION: Did all groups successfully observe the 3:1 cylinder-to-cone relationship in Activity 2A? If groups got results like 2.5 or 3.5, what measurement errors occurred — and would a digital simulation be a more reliable alternative for your classroom context?
3. COMPOSITE VOLUME (PART 3): How many groups successfully calculated the composite cylinder-hemisphere volume and connected it to the manufacturer's 5 000-litre claim? Groups that could not attempt Part 3 may need additional scaffolding on composite solids before Lesson 8 (which addresses the frustum).
4. DQB QUALITY: Review the sticky notes posted on the Driving Question Board. Do the green notes contain specific mathematical evidence, or are they vague ('we learned about volume')? Plan targeted prompting for Lesson 7 to raise the precision of DQB contributions.
5. MODEL PROGRESS: From the 5 models collected, did learners correctly represent the independence of surface area and volume? If most models show the two properties as connected (e.g., 'bigger surface area = bigger volume'), this is a critical misconception to address explicitly at the start of Lesson 7 using a counterexample — such as comparing a flat sheet (large surface area, small volume) with a sphere (compact shape, efficient volume).
6. PACING: The lesson has a high activity density (sand activity + reading + three-part calculation + DQB + model). If pacing was tight, consider assigning Part 3 of the Explain Challenge as a homework task reviewed at the start of Lesson 7, freeing time for deeper model-building in class.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed that filling a cone with sand and pouring it into a cylinder of equal base and height requires exactly 3 cones to fill the cylinder — providing concrete, physical evidence that the volume of a cone is one-third the volume of its corresponding cylinder. They also observed labelled diagrams of two grain stores (a rectangular prism and a cone) in Kitale, Trans-Nzoia County, with identical base widths and heights.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners learned the volume formulae for prisms (V = Base Area × h), pyramids and cones (V = ⅓ × Base Area × h), cylinders (V = πr²h), and spheres (V = 4/3 πr³). They discovered that the ⅓ factor arises because a cone or pyramid occupies exactly one-third of the space of a prism or cylinder with the same base and height. They learned to convert between m³ and litres (1 m³ = 1 000 L) and calculated that the cylindrical-hemispherical Kisumu tank holds approximately 5 030 litres — consistent with the manufacturer's 5 000-litre claim.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners explained that the Kitale farmer's rectangular grain store holds approximately 3.8 times more maize than the conical store with the same base and height, because the cone's tapering shape means it occupies only one-third of an equivalent cylinder's volume. They further explained that the Kisumu tank manufacturer can engineer equal volumes (5 000 litres) into very different shapes — cylindrical-hemispherical vs. frustum — confirming that volume and surface area are mathematically independent properties, and that shape choice affects material cost (surface area) without necessarily changing storage capacity (volume).

LESSONS.aresKeywords: volume of prisms, volume of pyramids, volume of cones, volume of spheres, composite solids, volume formulae, cylinder volume, cone volume, sphere volume, prism volume, volume vs surface area, 1/3 relationship cone cylinder, volume unit conversion, litres to cubic metres, grain store Kitale, water tank Kisumu, Siaya County, Trans-Nzoia County, maize storage Kenya, tank manufacturer Kenya, surface area and volume independent, CBE Grade 10 mathematics, Sub-Strand 2.4, solid geometry Kenya, KICD mathematics

LESSONS.title: Volume of Frustums of a Cone
LESSONS.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.4: Surface Area and Volume of Solids

slo.purpose: To enable learners to derive and apply the formula for the volume of a frustum of a cone in real-world contexts, specifically to verify the storage capacity of the frustum-shaped water tank from the anchoring phenomenon.
slo.knowledge: Learners will know that the volume of a frustum is derived by subtracting the volume of the smaller removed cone from the volume of the original larger cone, expressed as V = (1/3)πh(R² + Rr + r²), where R and r are the radii of the larger and smaller circular faces respectively and h is the perpendicular height of the frustum.
slo.skills: Learners will be able to: (a) derive the frustum volume formula from the difference of two cones; (b) identify the dimensions R, r, and h from a given frustum; (c) calculate the volume of a frustum using the derived formula; (d) convert between cubic centimetres/metres and litres to verify real-world capacity claims.
slo.attitudes: Learners will appreciate how mathematical derivation provides tools that engineers and manufacturers in Kenya use to make evidence-based design decisions that balance cost and functionality.
slo.keyInquiry: How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 6 learners calculated the surface area of the frustum tank and confirmed it uses less metal than the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank. A key question remained unanswered on the DQB: Does the frustum tank truly hold 5,000 litres? Lesson 7 equips learners with the volume formula for a frustum so they can compute the internal volume of the Kisumu factory's frustum tank, verify the manufacturer's 5,000-litre claim, and move one step closer to fully resolving the driving question.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used. If learners handle physical cone/frustum cut models, ensure edges are not sharp. Devices used to stream or view the YouTube video should be handled responsibly and returned to the charging station after use.

LESSONS.overview: Learners open by revisiting their DQB and recalling that the frustum tank's surface area is smaller than the composite tank's — but they have not yet verified the volume claim. Through a short prediction task, learners estimate how the volume of a frustum relates to the two cones involved. They then watch a curated YouTube video demonstrating the derivation of the frustum volume formula from the subtraction of cone volumes. Using structured notes, learners work through the algebraic derivation in pairs. They apply the formula to the Kisumu factory's frustum tank dimensions to confirm whether it holds 5,000 litres (≈ 5 m³), recording findings as evidence. The lesson closes with a DQB update and a cumulative model revision that now includes both surface area and volume comparisons for the frustum tank.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners individually study a labelled cross-section diagram of a frustum showing the original large cone (height H, radius R) and the small removed cone (height h_s, radius r). They respond in their exercise books to the prompt: 'The Kisumu factory's frustum tank is made by cutting the top off a large cone. Without calculating, predict: is the volume of the frustum closer to the volume of the big cone, exactly half of it, or something else? Write a sentence justifying your prediction using what you already know about cone volumes.' After 3 minutes of silent individual thinking, learners share predictions with a shoulder partner for 90 seconds.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the cross-section diagram on the board or screen. Say verbatim: 'Before we derive any formula today, I want your brain to commit to a prediction — this is how real engineers in Kisumu think before they calculate.' Circulate and read 3–4 exercise books silently. After partner sharing, cold-call 3 learners using a random name selector or row-by-row system. Ask each: 'What did you predict and why?' Allow 10 seconds of WAIT TIME after each response before commenting. Do NOT confirm or deny predictions — say: 'Hold that thought. The video we are about to watch will either confirm or challenge you.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction Journaling — by committing a written prediction before instruction, learners activate prior knowledge of cone volume (V = ⅓πr²h) and create a cognitive anchor. When the derivation later confirms or refines their prediction, the contrast drives deeper encoding of the frustum formula.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each learner has a written prediction with a justification referencing cone volume within the first 3 minutes. Teacher scans for misconceptions — e.g., learners who think the frustum volume equals the full cone volume (forgetting subtraction) or who cannot recall the cone volume formula. Note these learners for targeted support during the Observe phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners watch a teacher-selected YouTube video (approximately 8–10 minutes) demonstrating the derivation of the frustum volume formula. Before the video begins, each learner receives a structured note-taking frame with three columns: 'What I see/hear', 'Mathematical step', 'Question I have'. Learners pause the video at two teacher-designated timestamps — (1) after the setup of the two cones and (2) after the algebraic simplification — to discuss with their partner what they wrote. After the video, learners individually complete a 'Video Summary Strip': they write the frustum volume formula in their own words and label a blank frustum diagram with H, h_s, R, r, and h (perpendicular height of the frustum).
framework.teacherMoves: Before playing the video say verbatim: 'You are watching this video the way the Grade 10 students in Kisumu would watch a training video at the factory — with a purpose. Your job is to track every mathematical move the presenter makes.' Pause the video at the two designated timestamps. At Pause 1 ask: 'What are the two cones the presenter identified? Write their dimensions.' At Pause 2 ask: 'What operation connects the two cone volumes? Why subtraction?' Cold-call 2 learners at each pause — allow 10 seconds of WAIT TIME. After the video, circulate and check that every learner's diagram is correctly labelled before moving to the Explain phase.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Note-Taking with Designated Pauses — the three-column frame prevents passive watching by requiring simultaneous processing. The two pauses create retrieval checkpoints that consolidate partial understanding before the derivation continues, mimicking the 'chunking' technique used in engineering training programmes.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learner's Video Summary Strip shows the correct formula V_frustum = (1/3)πh(R² + Rr + r²) and a correctly labelled frustum diagram. Teacher checks 6–8 strips during the partner discussion pauses. Red flag: learners who write V = (1/3)πR²H − (1/3)πr²h_s but cannot simplify further — these learners need the algebraic bridge in Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in pairs through a three-part structured worksheet. PART A — Derivation Reconstruction: Given the expressions V_big = (1/3)πR²H and V_small = (1/3)πr²h_s, and the similar-triangles relationship H/R = h_s/r (therefore h_s = Hr/R), learners substitute and simplify to derive V_frustum = (1/3)πh(R² + Rr + r²), where h = H − h_s. Teacher guides the class through the algebraic steps on the board, pausing for pairs to complete each line before revealing the next. PART B — Worked Example (Kisumu Factory Tank): The frustum tank has dimensions: R = 0.9 m, r = 0.5 m, h = 2.1 m. Learners calculate its volume and convert to litres (1 m³ = 1,000 L) to verify whether it holds 5,000 L. PART C — Extension: A posho mill owner in Kericho stores maize flour in a frustum-shaped silo with R = 1.2 m, r = 0.7 m, h = 3.0 m. Calculate the volume in m³ and the mass of flour it can hold if the density of maize flour is approximately 600 kg/m³.
framework.teacherMoves: For Part A, write each algebraic step on the board one line at a time. Say: 'Before I write the next line, complete it in your book. You have 45 seconds.' Use WAIT TIME of 45 seconds consistently. Cold-call one pair per step to read their line aloud — rotate through the room systematically. If a pair gives an incorrect step, say: 'Interesting — can you tell me where you started from?' rather than correcting immediately. For Part B, after pairs calculate, ask: 'Hands up if your answer is between 4,900 L and 5,100 L.' Count hands aloud and record on the board. Then cold-call one pair with the correct answer to narrate their full working. For Part C, circulate to the 3–4 pairs identified during the Observe phase and provide a scaffolded hint card showing the density formula.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Derivation Reconstruction with Algebraic Chaining — rather than presenting the formula as a given, learners rebuild it step-by-step using the subtraction of cones and similar-triangles reasoning. This strategy connects the frustum formula back to prior knowledge (cone volume, similarity) and prevents rote memorisation. The Kisumu factory context keeps every algebraic step tethered to the anchoring phenomenon.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator for Part B: Learner correctly calculates V ≈ (1/3) × π × 2.1 × (0.81 + 0.45 + 0.25) = (1/3) × π × 2.1 × 1.51 ≈ 3.328 m³ ≈ 3,328 L — which is less than 5,000 L, prompting a rich discussion about whether the manufacturer's claim is accurate or whether additional dimensions are needed. Teacher notes which pairs reach this conclusion independently as evidence of high-order reasoning.

framework.learnerExperience: Each pair receives one sticky note or card. They write one statement beginning with 'We now know…' about the frustum tank's volume and one question beginning with 'We still wonder…' about the driving question. Pairs post their cards on the class DQB under two columns: 'VOLUME EVIDENCE' and 'REMAINING QUESTIONS'. The class reads the board silently for 60 seconds, then the teacher facilitates a 5-minute whole-class discussion connecting today's volume finding to the surface area evidence from Lesson 6. Learners update their individual DQB tracking sheets, marking Lesson 7 as complete.
framework.teacherMoves: Stand beside the DQB and read two or three 'We still wonder' cards aloud. Say verbatim: 'These questions are gold — they tell us exactly what we still need to investigate. Look at the board: we now know both the surface area AND the volume of the frustum tank. What are we still missing to fully answer our driving question?' Allow 10 seconds of WAIT TIME. Cold-call 3 learners. Guide discussion toward: (1) the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank's volume has not yet been calculated, and (2) a direct numerical comparison of both surface areas and both volumes is still needed. Say: 'Add those to your tracking sheet as the target for Lessons 8 and 9.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DQB Evidence Mapping — posting findings under 'VOLUME EVIDENCE' makes the accumulation of evidence across lessons visible and spatial. When learners see that Lessons 6 and 7 together now cover surface area AND volume of the frustum, they can identify the remaining gap (the composite tank's volume) as the next investigative target, reinforcing scientific reasoning habits.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each pair's 'We now know…' statement correctly references a volume value (in litres or m³) for the frustum tank and connects it to the driving question. Teacher checks that at least 80% of pairs have identified 'the composite tank's volume' as a remaining question, confirming readiness to proceed to Lesson 8.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their cumulative 'Tank Comparison Model' — a table and annotated diagram they have been building since Lesson 1. Today they add a new row to the Volume section of the table for the frustum tank, entering the value calculated in Phase 3. They also annotate their frustum diagram with the formula V = (1/3)πh(R² + Rr + r²) and the derived value in litres. In the 'Explanation' column of their model, they write one sentence connecting volume to the anchoring phenomenon: 'The frustum tank's volume of approximately _____ litres [confirms / does not confirm] the manufacturer's 5,000-litre claim because…'. Learners who finish early begin a side-by-side sketch comparing the frustum and composite tanks, labelling all known dimensions and calculated values from Lessons 1–7.
framework.teacherMoves: Circulate and read the 'Explanation' column entries as learners write. Look for three quality indicators: (1) the correct volume value is cited, (2) the word 'because' is followed by mathematical reasoning, and (3) the phenomenon (Kisumu factory, 5,000-litre claim) is explicitly named. If a learner's explanation is vague, prompt: 'You said the tank holds less than 5,000 litres — what does that mean for the manufacturer's claim? What would a factory engineer in Kisumu need to check?' Collect 4–5 model sheets to review after class and return with written feedback before Lesson 8.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision — the Tank Comparison Model functions as a running scientific model that grows in explanatory power with each lesson. Adding the frustum volume today alongside the surface area from Lesson 6 allows learners to see, in one visual artefact, how shape affects both properties simultaneously. This mirrors how engineers maintain and update technical drawings as new data is gathered.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: The learner's model now contains: (a) correctly labelled frustum dimensions (R, r, h); (b) the formula written in full; (c) a calculated volume value with unit conversion shown; (d) a written explanation linking the value to the manufacturer's claim. Teacher uses these four criteria as a checklist when reviewing collected sheets. Learners missing criterion (d) are flagged for a brief individual check-in at the start of Lesson 8.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After the lesson, reflect on the following questions and record your notes before Lesson 8:
1. DERIVATION DEPTH — Did the majority of pairs successfully reconstruct the algebraic derivation in Part A, or did many learners copy the steps without understanding the similar-triangles link? If the latter, plan a 5-minute retrieval starter for Lesson 8 that re-asks: 'Why is h_s/r = H/R?'
2. VOLUME RESULT — When learners calculated the frustum tank's volume and found it was significantly less than 5,000 litres using the given dimensions, how did they respond? Did they question the dimensions provided, or did they accept the result uncritically? Use this as a diagnostic of mathematical confidence and critical thinking.
3. DQB QUALITY — Were the 'We still wonder' cards specific enough to drive Lesson 8? If most cards were vague (e.g., 'we wonder about the other tank'), prompt more precise questioning at the start of Lesson 8: 'What specific calculation do we need to do next, and which formula will we use?'
4. PACING — The algebraic derivation in Phase 3 is the most cognitively demanding section. If the class ran short on time, Phase 5 model building can begin as a homework task, with learners completing the 'Explanation' column at home and sharing at the start of Lesson 8.
5. DIFFERENTIATION — Note which pairs required the scaffolded hint card for Part C. Plan to seat these learners near the board during Lesson 8's composite volume derivation so they receive more proximal support.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners watched a YouTube video showing how the volume of a frustum is derived by subtracting the smaller removed cone's volume from the larger original cone's volume, pausing at two designated points to record and discuss the mathematical steps.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: The volume of a frustum of a cone is given by V = (1/3)πh(R² + Rr + r²), derived from the difference of two cone volumes using similar-triangle proportionality. Learners applied this formula to calculate the volume of the Kisumu factory's frustum-shaped water tank and converted the result to litres to evaluate the manufacturer's 5,000-litre storage claim.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners explained, in their cumulative Tank Comparison Models, how the calculated volume of the frustum tank either confirms or challenges the manufacturer's advertised capacity of 5,000 litres, and identified that the composite cylinder-hemisphere tank's volume must still be calculated before a full comparison — and a complete answer to the driving question — can be made.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: frustum volume, cone subtraction, similar triangles, volume formula derivation, surface area comparison, water tank capacity, litres conversion, Kisumu factory, composite solids, Tank Comparison Model, DQB update, Grade 10 mathematics Kenya, CBE mathematics, 2.4 surface area and volume

LESSONS.title: Volume of Frustums of a Pyramid
LESSONS.substrand: Sub-Strand 2.4: Surface Area and Volume of Solids

slo.purpose: To enable learners to derive and apply the formula for the volume of a frustum of a pyramid in real-world contexts related to construction and agribusiness in Kenya.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to derive the formula for the volume of a frustum of a pyramid using the subtraction of pyramid volumes, and apply it to calculate the volume of frustum-shaped structures such as grain stores and architectural bases.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) derive the formula V = (h/3)(A₁ + A₂ + √(A₁A₂)) for the volume of a frustum of a pyramid; (b) calculate the volume of frustum-shaped solids given relevant dimensions; (c) solve real-life problems involving frustum grain stores and construction structures in Kenya.
slo.attitudes: The learner appreciates how mathematical reasoning about frustum volumes supports efficient and cost-effective design decisions made by engineers, architects, and agribusiness professionals in Kenya.
slo.keyInquiry: How do we apply surface area and volume of solids in day-to-day activities? How does the shape of a solid affect its surface area and volume?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 7, learners investigated the volume of a frustum of a cone. In Lesson 8, learners extend this understanding to frustums of pyramids — the shape used in many traditional grain stores (mapunjas) and modern architectural plinths across Kenya. By deriving and applying the pyramid-frustum volume formula, learners gather new evidence to compare the frustum-tank design at the Kisumu factory with other frustum-shaped containers, further advancing their answer to the driving question about why shape affects material use and storage capacity.
slo.safetyNotes: No physical hazards anticipated. Ensure all learners have access to a calculator. If the YouTube video is played, confirm the audio volume is appropriate for the classroom setting and that the video has been pre-screened for content suitability.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by recalling the cone-frustum volume formula from Lesson 7 and predicting whether a similar approach will work for pyramid frustums. They then watch a curated YouTube video demonstrating the derivation of the pyramid-frustum volume formula. Through guided sensemaking, learners derive the formula V = (h/3)(A₁ + A₂ + √(A₁A₂)) by subtracting the volume of the smaller removed pyramid from the larger whole pyramid. They apply the formula to two Kenya-context problems: (1) a frustum-shaped grain store (mapunjas) used by a maize farmer in the Rift Valley, and (2) a frustum-shaped concrete base for a water tower in Siaya County — directly echoing the anchoring phenomenon. Learners update their Driving Question Boards and revise their cumulative models to incorporate pyramid-frustum volume alongside cone-frustum volume from the previous lesson.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a diagram of a frustum of a square pyramid (wider square base, narrower square top, vertical height h) labelled as a traditional mapunjas grain store from the Rift Valley. They are asked: 'This frustum grain store has a square base of side 2 m, a square top of side 1 m, and a height of 1.5 m. Predict: do you think the formula for its volume will look the same as the cone-frustum formula we used in Lesson 7? Write your prediction and your reasoning in your exercise book. You have 3 minutes — work independently.' Learners write individual predictions, then share with a shoulder partner for 1 minute.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the diagram of the mapunjas frustum on the board or projector. Say: 'Yesterday we found the volume of a cone frustum — the same shape used in those Kisumu storage tanks. Today we are looking at a different frustum: one cut from a pyramid, not a cone. Before we do any mathematics, I want you to think and predict.' Distribute the diagram handout. Set a 3-minute silent individual writing timer. After 3 minutes, say: 'Now turn to your shoulder partner and share your prediction. You have 1 minute.' After partner share, cold-call 3 learners: 'Wanjiku, what did you predict and why?' — 'Otieno, do you agree or disagree with Wanjiku? Why?' — 'Achieng, what was different about your reasoning?' Apply 10–15 seconds of WAIT TIME after each question before prompting. Record key prediction phrases on the board under the heading: 'Our Predictions — Pyramid Frustum Volume'. Do NOT confirm or correct predictions yet.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share Prediction: Learners first commit to individual reasoning (reducing anchoring bias), then test their thinking against a peer, then hear three contrasting views publicly. This activates prior knowledge of cone frustums and surfaces misconceptions (e.g., 'the formula must use πr²') before instruction begins.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Learners' written predictions show engagement with the structural analogy to cone frustums. Listen for whether learners correctly identify that the circular cross-section (πr²) will be replaced by a square or rectangular area. Flag learners who predict a completely unrelated formula — they may need scaffolding during the derivation phase.
